Definition 2

title: Risk: Expected Loss Definition
body: Definition 1
“Risk” can refer to a situation in which there is an expected loss
Example
Assume two identical homes in equally attractive locations, both worth $100K
Announcement that a meteor will strike the earth and completely destroy one home within the next week with a probability of 0.1 (10%)
Expected Loss for Home in Impact Zone = $100,000 x 0.1 = $10,000
Expected Loss for Home NOT in Impact Zone = $100,000 x 0 = $0
Q: How much would you pay for home in the impact zone?
A: Rational person would pay at least $10,000 less (i.e., pay only $90K or less: (Value of House) – (Expected Loss) = $100,000 – [0.1($100,000)+0.9($0)] = $100,000 – 10,000 - $ = $90,000

title: Risk: Variability Definition
body: Definition 2
“Risk” can also refer to a situation in which there is variability around an expected loss/result
Example
Value of house NOT in impact zone = $100K with certainty
House in impact zone worth either $0 or $100K
Such a high degree of uncertainty will likely impose additional costs on the owner
Many potential buyers would offer less than $90K (i.e., maybe only $85K to reflect their unease with the uncertainty. How much that penalty is depends on the buyer’s degree of risk aversion.
Q: Are you risk averse?
A: Generally yes, but sometimes not. Also, humans are historically and notoriously terrible at evaluating most risks.

title: types of risk
body: Common Categorizations of Risk

title: Types of Risk: Pure vs. Speculative
body: Pure Risk: A chance of loss or no loss, but no chance of gain
Examples: A building burns or doesn’t burn; Car crashes or doesn’t
Always undesirable; Neither outcome produces a gain
Speculative Risk: Involves a chance of gain
Examples
Price Risk: Risk associated with change in price of inputs as well as market price for completed outputs
Credit Risk: Risk that customers and parties to whom the company has lent money delay or fail to make promised payments
Market Risk: Risk associated with change in price of financial securities (e.g., stocks and bonds)
Interest Rate Risk: Risk associated with value of a security because of changes in interest rates (e.g., If Fed raises rates, value of existing bonds may fall)
Liquidity Risk: Risk associated with being able to easily liquidate an investment
Exchange Rate Risk: Risk associated with the value of assets (financial or physical) based on fluctuations in exchange rates

title: Types of Risk: Objective vs. Subjective
body: Objective Risk: A risk where the degree of variation in uncertain outcomes can be measured (quantified) based on facts, data and analysis
Examples
Hurricane Risk: Based on actual history of insured losses, catastrophe modeling, climate science, etc.
Mortality Risk: Based on data associated with decades of statistics related to death statistics
Subjective Risk: Perceived degree of risk is based on an individual’s (or an organization’s) opinion
Some people refuse to fly and drive instead, even though flying (commercially) is far safer
People grossly overestimate their odds of being killed in many ways: shark attack, terrorism, flying, contracting certain diseases (e.g., Zika, Ebola)
People grossly underestimate many risks: home being flooded; likelihood of death from smoking or being overweight or not wearing a motorcycle helmet
NOTE: Bias and lack of awareness increase subjectivity

Chance of Loss vs. Objective Risk
body: Chance of loss is the probability that an event that causes a loss will occur.
Objective risk is the relative variation of actual loss from expected loss
The chance of loss may be identical for two different groups, but objective risk may be quite different!
City: Philadelphia · # homes: 10,000 · Average # Fires/Yr.: 100 · Range in # of Fires/Yr.: 75 – 125 · Chance of Fire: 1% · Objective Risk: 25%
City: Los Angeles · # homes: 10,000 · Average # Fires/Yr.: 100 · Range in # of Fires/Yr.: 90 - 110 · Chance of Fire: 1% · Objective Risk: 10%
Question: Which city is riskier for an insurer? Philadephia or Los Angeles? Why?

title: Diversification and the Volatility of Stock Portfolios
body: If hold a portfolio of firms with only non-diversifiable risk, then risk is the same no matter how many stocks are held (e.g. own stocks only of earthquake insurers)
If hold portfolio of firms with only of purely diversifiable risk, then risk is essentially completely eliminated as number stocks held increases
Typical stocks have a blend of both systemic and unsystemic risks

title: Application: The Effect of Diversification on Portfolio Volatility (2-Stock Portfolio)
Application: Diversification in Stock Portfolios
body: Unsystematic (Diversifiable) vs Systematic Risk (Undiversifiable)
Over any given period of time, the risk of holding a stock is that the dividends plus the difference purchase and sale price will be different than expected, which make holding the stock risky
What accounts for returns to differ from expectations? NEWS (i.e., Information)
Stock prices are impacted by two types of news
Company or Industry-Specific News
Market-Wide News
Unsystematic Risk (e.g., FB’s problems)

title: Application: Diversification in Stock Portfolios
body: Unsystematic Versus Systematic Risk
Stock prices are impacted by 2 types of news
Company or Industry-Specific News: Unsystematic Risk

Good or bad news about a company (or industry) itself. Example: firm might announce that a newly introduced product has succeeded in gaining market share (FB’s data problems, modest (e.g., 10%) aluminum tariff)
Market-Wide News: Systematic Risk
News that affects the economy overall and therefore affects all stocks (Fed raises interest rates, Trump’s election, Trade War)

title: Application: Diversification in Stock Portfolios
body: Diversifiable Risk and the Risk Premium
The risk premium of a stock is not affected by its diversifiable, unsystematic risk
In other words, you’re not going to get an extra return (i.e., risk premium) for holding a stock whose risk can be diversified
The risk premium for diversifiable risk is zero
Investors are not compensated for holding unsystematic risk

title: Additional Common Definitions of Risk
body: Traditional Definitions of Risk: Uncertainty concerning the occurrence of a loss
ALSO an FYI: In the insurance industry, the term “risk” is also used to identify the property or life that is being considered for insurance

The term risk is used in situations where the probabilities of possible outcomes are known or can be estimated with some degree of precision (e.g., prob. of dying at a given age)

topic: Types of Business Risk & Risk Management Methods
title: Techniques for Managing Risk
body: Risk (Loss) Control refers to techniques that reduce the frequency of losses
Avoidance: e.g., oil driller avoids drilling within 5 miles of homes to avoid the possibility of groundwater contamination
Loss prevention refers to activities to reduce the frequency of losses (e.g., driver safety training, anti-harassment training, tech)
Loss Reduction refers to activities to reduce the severity (cost) of losses
Duplication: e.g., routinely back up servers
Separation: e.g., don’t locate all of your manufacturing facilities at the same site
Diversification: e.g., don’t depend on a single supplier for a key component

topic: Types of Business Risk & Risk Management Methods
title: Internal Risk Reduction
body: Diversification: Diversification of business activities
i.e., “Don’t put all of your eggs in one basket”
Diversify in terms of products, geography, suppliers, customers, etc. (e.g., Apple shifting some iPhone production out China to India, Vietnam)
Portfolio/asset diversification for financial firms
Invest in Information: To obtain superior forecasts in terms of expected losses (works for speculative and pure risks)

topic: Types of Business Risk & Risk Management Methods
title: Techniques for Managing Risk (Continued)
body: Risk (Loss) Financing: Retention refers to techniques that provide for payment of losses after they occur through self-funding
Retention means that an individual or business firm retains part or all of the losses that can result from a given risk.
Active Retention means that an individual is aware of the risk and deliberately plans to retain all or part of it
Passive Retention means risks may be unknowingly retained because of ignorance, indifference, or laziness

topic: Types of Business Risk & Risk Management Methods

body: Loss Financing (cont’d)
Self Insurance is a special form of planned retention by which part or all of a given loss exposure is retained by the firm; Losses are retained using methods typically employed by insurers.
Captive Insurance An insurance subsidiary of a company whose primary business is not insurance. Professionally managed.

A transfer of risk by contract, such as through a hold-harmless clause in a contract
Hedging is a technique for transferring the risk of unfavorable price fluctuations to a speculator
Incorporation of a business firm transfers to the creditors the risk of having insufficient assets and other potential liabilities

Transfer of a pure risk to an insurer
Insurance is pooling technique and is used to spread the losses of the few over the entire group
The risk may be reduced by application of the law of large numbers

topic: Types of Business Risk & Risk Management Methods
title: 5 Components of the Cost of Loss
body: Expected Losses: Includes both direct and indirect losses. In auto manufacturer example direct losses include cost of litigation and settlements as well product recall and replacement. Indirect costs include lost sales if production has to be slowed or company’s reputation is damaged.
Cost of Loss Control: Reflects costs of increased precautionary measures (e.g., additional testing)

Cost of Loss Financing: Includes cost of self-insurance, the profit and expense “loading” in insurance premiums (actual premium is part of firm’s expected losses), transaction costs in negotiating, arranging hedging and other contractual risk transfers
Cost of Internal Risk Reduction Methods: Includes transaction costs associated with achieving diversification and managing diverse set of activities as well as the cost of information
Cost of Residual Uncertainty: In general, there will always be some uncertainty “left over” even after the selection and implementation of Loss Control, Loss Financing and Internal Risk Reduction
Can affect, for example shareholders, pay demands of executives

topic: Types of Business Risk & Risk Management Methods
title: Four Methods of Loss (Risk) Financing
body: Retention: Situation when an individual/business retains the obligation to pay some or all of the losses
Insurance: Insurance company pays losses specified in a contract in exchange for a premium
Hedging: Refers to the use of derivatives contracts such as futures, forwards, options and swaps
Individuals, small businesses rarely use this option
Larger businesses do to guard against such things a commodity price fluctuation

Example: Fuel is the second largest cost for airlines. Airlines routinely hedge against fuel price fluctuation by entering into futures contract with a fuel producer (refinery) that guarantees delivery of a specific quantity of fuel in the future at a price established today
Other Contractual Risk Transfers: Parties doing business with one another routinely include in their agreements language about which party shall assume liability (e.g., hold harmless agreement)

title: Risk Is Costly—So Is Management of that Risk
body: Example
Automobile Manufacturer: Vehicles always run the risk of being manufactured with defects in parts, assembly—and increasingly software.
To minimize this risk, which can lead to expensive individual or class action lawsuits and fines, car manufacturers (and their suppliers) exhaustively test components and test crash vehicles
Safety equipment in vehicles also makes them safer, but is costly
These testing programs and safety equipment are expensive and time consuming and add significantly to the cost of vehicles
The Point: Risk and Risk Reduction are both costly so our objective is minimize the cost of risk to business, the individual or to society (depending on the entity under consideration)
Note that actions minimizing costs to society may differ from actions minimizing cost to the firm/individual

title: Definition of Insurance
body: Insurance: The pooling of fortuitous losses by transfer of such risks to insurers, who agree to indemnify (i.e., pay) insureds for such losses, to provide other pecuniary (i.e., monetary) benefits on their occurrence, or to render services connected with the risk

title: Basic Characteristics of Insurance
body: Pooling of Losses
Pooling involves spreading losses incurred by the few over the entire group
Risk reduction is based on the Law of Large Numbers

According to the Law of Large Numbers, the greater the number of exposures, the more closely will the actual results approach the probable results that are expected from an infinite number of exposures.

title: Basic Characteristics of Insurance (Continued)
body: Example of Expect Loss and SD w/o Pooling
Two business owners own identical buildings valued at $50,000
There is a 10% chance each building will be destroyed by a peril in any year (and therefore a 90% it won’t be destroyed)
Loss to either building is an independent event
Expected value and standard deviation of the loss for each owner individually is

title: Basic Characteristics of Insurance (Continued)
body: Example, continued, but WITH Pooling
If the owners instead pool (combine) their loss exposures, and each agrees to pay an equal share of any loss that might occur
Neither building destroyed: 0.9 x 0.9 = 0.81
Bldg. A Destroyed, Bldg. B no loss:	0.1 x 0.9 = .09
Bldg. A no loss, Bldg. B Destroyed:	0.9 x 0.1 = .09
Both Bldgs Destroyed: 0.1 x 0.1 = .01
As additional individuals are added to the pool, the standard deviation continues to decline while the expected value of the loss remains unchanged
$25,000 = $50,000/2 = shared amount if one structure or the other is destroyed

title: Basic Characteristics of Insurance (Continued)
body: Payment of Fortuitous Losses
A fortuitous loss is one that is unforeseen, unexpected, and occur as a result of chance
Risk Transfer
A pure risk is transferred from the insured to the insurer, who typically is in a stronger financial position
Indemnification
The insured is restored to his or her approximate financial position prior to the occurrence of the loss

title: 6 Characteristics of an Ideally Insurable Risk
body: Large number of exposure units
to predict average loss based on the law of large numbers
Accidental and unintentional loss
to assure random occurrence of events
Determinable and measurable loss
to determine how much should be paid

title: 6 Characteristics of an Ideally Insurable Risk (Continued)
body: No Exceedingly Catastrophic Loss
to allow the pooling technique to work (i.e., not all policyholders can suffer the same loss)
exposures to catastrophic loss can be managed by using reinsurance, dispersing coverage over a large geographic area, or using financial instruments, such as catastrophe bonds
Calculable Chance of Loss
to establish a premium that is sufficient to pay all claims and expenses and yields a profit during the policy period
Economically Feasible Premium
so people can afford to purchase the policy
For insurance to be an attractive purchase, the premiums paid must be substantially less than the face value, or amount, of the policy

title: Direct vs. Indirect Losses
body: Direct Losses: Losses (with financial consequences) that arise as the immediate consequence of an adverse event

Indirect Losses: Losses that arise as the result of a direct loss that has occurred.
Indirect losses can be very large, especially for businesses
If a business is damage by a fire, it may be closed for repairs for days of weeks, resulting in a loss of income and potentially addition expenses
For personal risks, indirect losses can be costly too.

title: Risk Management and Societal Welfare
body: From a societal standpoint, the question (and goal) is how risky activities and risk management by businesses and individuals can be arranged to minimize the total costs to society (all of its members).
Minimizing total risk to society yields an efficient level of risk.

The efficient level of risk requires that individuals businesses pursue risk management activities until the marginal reduction in the expected cost of losses equals the marginal cost of these risk management activities

title: Risk Management and Societal Welfare

Example 1: In absence of workplace safety regulation and workers compensation statutes, businesses would have a greatly reduced incentive to protect workers on the job from the risk of injury or death.

This is clearly not optimal from a societal perspective given the high cost of injuries, workers unable to earn income if injured, families’ loss of wage earners if disabled/killed on the job, plus many other indirect costs. Can you name a few?

title: Firm Value Maximization and the Cost of Risk
body: Basic Equation
Cost of Risk = (Value w/o Risk) – (Value w/Risk) The firm’s value w/o risk is hypothetical and represents the value of the firm when uncertainty in cash flows is reduced to zero—and at zero cost.
We can restate this equation to represent firm’s value to shareholders
Value w/Risk = (Value w/o Risk) – (Cost of Risk) Implies that the value of the firm is maximized when its cost of risk is minimized

As a practical matter, businesses (esp. small businesses) will not attempt to quantify all risks, but management still needs to understand risks in general and how their magnitude will be impacted by risk management efforts.

title: Firm Value Maximization and the Cost of Risk
body: Value of a Business: Determined primarily by expected magnitude, timing and risk (variability) associated with future net cash flows (inflows minus outflows) that will be available to provide shareholders (owners) with a return on their investment
Volatility (Risk) in Cash Flows: It is a fundamental business principle of business valuation that increased risk reduces firm value and increases the expected return required by investors.

title: Which Firm Will Have a Higher Valuation?
body: Firm A: Stock pays $100 dividend/share per year in perpetuity with an interest rate of 5% and a standard deviation in those returns of 10%
Firm B: Stock pays $100 dividend/share per year in perpetuity with an interest rate of 5% and a standard deviation in those returns of 15%?
Solution: Value (i.e. present value) of this investment in both cases is D/r = (Dividend/Interest Rate) = $100/.05 = $2,000
But Firm A will be worth more to risk averse investors because there’s less volatility in the stream of earnings!

title: Law of Large Numbers
body: The Law of Large Numbers is the mathematical foundation of insurance.
LLN: As sample size increase  infinity, sample mean population mean
Average losses for a random sample of n exposure units will follow a normal distribution because of the Central Limit Theorem.
Regardless of the population distribution, the distribution of sample means will approach the normal distribution as the sample size increases.
The standard error of the sampling distribution can be reduced by increasing the sample size
In Plain English: If I repeatedly sample from the same population, the distribution of the means themselves in normally distributed

title: Exhibit A2.1 Sampling Distribution Versus Sample Size
body: Central Limit Theorem: States that as the sample size increases, the sampling distribution of the mean approaches a standard normal distribution, regardless of the population distribution

title: Standard Error of the Sampling Distribution Versus Sample Size (Exhibit A2.2 from text)
body: Mathematically—the standard error of the distribution mean decreases as the number of insured units (n) increases

title: Law of Large Numbers (Continued)
body: When an insurer increases the size of the sample of insureds (i.e., policyholders)
Underwriting risk increases, because more insured units could suffer a loss (i.e., the insurer could lose more).
But, underwriting risk does not increase proportionately. It increases by the square root of the increase in the sample size.
There is “safety in numbers” for insurers!
When an investor increases the # stocks in their portfolio
Investment risk increases, because more stocks could suffer a loss (i.e., the investor could lose more).
But, investment risk does not increase proportionately. It increases by the square root of the increase in the portfolio size (if equally weighted).
There is “safety in numbers” for investors too! What is this effect called?
Diversification

title: Maximum Probable Loss (MPL)
body: Maximum Probable Loss: Usually describes a loss distribution

In the figure below representing annual liability losses, if the area to the right of $20 mill represents 5% of the area in the loss distribution, then $20 mill is the MPL at the 5% level. Losses will be < $20 mill 95% of the time.
The MPL at the 1% level is $30 mill. Implies losses will be < $30 mill 99% of the time

Since this is a loss distribution, higher numbers are bad; lower numbers are good. Insurers and RMs are very focused on limiting their MPL. Will likely buy reinsurance to limit losses beyond a certain threshold.

title: Value at Risk (VaR)
body: Value at Risk: VaR describes the probability distribution for the value of a portfolio or the value of a firm subject to loss, usually over a specified time interval
If Value at Risk at the 5% level for the next month is $20 million, then
Prob(loss in portfolio value ≥ $20 million) = 0.05
In words, there is 5% chance that the portfolio will lose more $20 million over the next month

title: Value at Risk (VAR)
body: VAR is similar to maximum probable loss, except it is usually applied to the value of a portfolio (e.g., stocks)
Key distinction is that the VAR refers to the change (reduction) in portfolio value from the expected portfolio value, at some level of probability.
If the Value at Risk at the 5% level for the next month equals $20 million, then
Prob (change in portfolio value < -$20 million) = 0.05
In words, there is 5% chance that the portfolio will lose more than $20 million over the next month

title: Value at Risk
body: Example
Assume VAR at the 5% level =$5 million
And VAR at the 1% level = $7.5 million
Since this is a distribution of changes in portfolio values, higher values are good

title: Value at Risk (VaR)

In other words, $5 mill is the monthly VaR for this portfolio at the 5% level. Losses will be < $5 mill 95% of the time.
The VaR at the 1% level in this portfolio is $7.5 mill meaning that the probability that the portfolio will lose more than $7.5 mill is 1%.

title: A Definition of Insurance
body: Insurance is the pooling of fortuitous losses by transfer to an insurer, who agrees to indemnify insureds for such losses and render services connected with the risk.
Insurance deals with risks that are pure, fortuitous, particular and diversifiable.

title: Main Contracting Costs In Running A Pool
body: Distribution Costs: To add participants to the pool

title: Premium Payments Versus Assessments
body: Today, policyholders pay an “ex ante” premium
Assessments are “ex post” payments
Generally, premium payments are made to the insurance company, without giving the insured the right to assess.
That is, the insurer obtains revenue prior to paying claims.
Advantage to the insurer: Positive cash flow and ability to invest the “float”.
Disadvantage: Cost of goods sold not known at the time of pricing. This makes pricing “risky”.

Predictability decreases with correlation in losses

title: Law of Large Numbers
body: The greater the number of exposures (e.g., people of businesses insured), the more closely will the actual result approach the expected result.
In other words, the mean of the sample we have approaches the true mean of the population as the number of exposures approaches infinity

title: The Law of Large Numbers
body: Insurance companies rely on the Law of Large Numbers to ensure accuracy when setting rates and premiums
They expect losses to occur.
Their major concern is a deviation between actual losses and expected losses.
By insuring large numbers of people/businesses, insurance companies reduce uncertainty (risk).
The risk to the whole becomes less than the sum of its parts.

title: Central Limit Theorem
body: The central limit theorem tells us that using the normal distribution can be appropriate if we are analyzing a sum of independent identically distributed random variables even if these random variables are not normally distributed.
Stated more specifically, in random samples of n observations, the distribution of the sample means will be a normal distribution.
And, the mean of the sample distribution will equal the mean of the population distribution!

title: Central Limit Theorem (cont.)
body: The standard error of the sample mean will be equal to the standard error of the population divided by the square root of n.
The standard error of the sampling distribution can be reduced simply by increasing the sample size.

title: Standard Error of the Sampling Distribution Versus Sample Size

title: Definition of Pooling
body: Pooling: Spreading of losses incurred by the few over the entire group so that the average loss is substituted for actual loss.
Concept was understood even by ancient civilizations
Achieves diversification (so long as risks are not perfectly correlated)
Pooling of losses is at the core of what insurers do

title: Risk Reduction From Pooling - Effect of Positive Correlation
body: Zero spread of risk—no diversification, so pooling provides no benefit
Partial (usually substantial) spread of risk—some diversification, so pooling provides some benefit
Total or near-total spread of risk—large benefits from diversification, so pooling benefits are maximized

Considers all risks across the organization and the strategic implications of the risks

body: Financial Risk Management: refers to the identification, analysis, and treatment of speculative financial risks
Commodity price risk
Interest rate risk
Currency exchange rate risk
Financial risks can be managed with capital market instruments

title: Exhibit 4.1: Hedging a Commodity Price Risk Using Futures Contracts
body: A corn grower estimates in May that he will harvest 20,000 bushels of corn by December.
The price on futures contracts for December corn is $4.90 per bushel.
Corn futures contracts are traded in 5000 bushel units
How can he hedge the risk that the price of corn will be lower at harvest time?

title: Exhibit 4.1: Hedging a Commodity Price Risk Using Futures Contracts (Continued)
body: He would sell four contracts in May totaling 20,000 bushels in the futures market.
In December, he would buy four contracts to offset his futures position.
If the market price of corn drops to $4.50 per bushel, cost is 20,000 x $4.50 = 90,000
If the market price of corn increases to $5.00 per bushel, cost is 20,000 x $5.00 = $100,000

title: Exhibit 4.1: Hedging a Commodity Price Risk Using Futures Contracts (Continued)
body: Note: it doesn’t matter whether the price of corn has increased or decreased by December.
If Price is $4.50 in December
Revenue from sale — $90,000
Sale of four contracts at $4.90 in May — $98,000
Purchase of four contracts at $4.50 in December — $90,000
Gain on futures transaction — $8,000
Total revenue — $98,000

title: Exhibit 4.1: Hedging a Commodity Price Risk Using Futures Contracts (Continued)
body: By using futures contracts and ignoring transaction costs, he has locked in total revenue of $98,000.
If Price is $5.00 in December
Revenue from sale — $100,000
Sale of four contracts at $4.90 in May — $98,000
Purchase of four contracts at $5.00 in December — $100,000
Loss on futures transaction — ($2,000)
Total revenue — $98,000

title: Exhibit 4.2: Using Options to Protect Against Stock Price Movements
body: Options on stocks can be used to protect against adverse stock price movements.
A call option gives the owner the right to buy shares (100 shares in our example) of stock at a given price during a specified period.
A put option gives the owner the right to sell (100) shares of stock at a given price during a specified period.
One option strategy is to buy put options to protect against a decline in the price of stock that is already owned.

title: Exhibit 4.2: Using Options to Protect Against Stock Price Movements (Continued)
body: Consider someone who owns 100 shares of a stock priced at $43 per share.
To reduce the risk of a price decline, she buys a put option with a strike (exercise) price of $40.
If the price of the stock increases, she has lost the purchase price of the option (called the premium), but the stock price has increased.

title: Exhibit 4.2: Using Options to Protect Against Stock Price Movements (Continued)
body: But what if the price of the stock declines, say to $33 per share?
Without the put option, the stock owner has lost $10 ($43–$33) per share on paper.
With the put option, she has the right to sell 100 shares at $40 per share. Thus, the option is “in the money” by $7 per share ($40–$33), ignoring the option premium.

title: The Changing Scope of Risk Management
body: An integrated risk management program is a technique that combines coverage for pure and speculative risks in the same contract
Some organizations have created a Chief Risk Officer (CRO) position
The chief risk officer is responsible for the treatment of pure and speculative risks faced by the organization
CRO is an increasingly important role in major corporations

title: ENTERPRISE RISK MANAGEMENT & RELATED TOPICS
body: The Science and Process of Managing Risk

title: Enterprise Risk Management
body: Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) is a comprehensive risk management program that addresses all risks faced by the corporation-pure risks, speculative financial risks, strategic risks, operational risks, and other risks.
Strategic risk refers to uncertainty regarding an organization’s goals and objectives
Operational risks develop out of business operations, such as manufacturing
As long as risks are not (strongly) positively correlated, the combination of these risks in a single program reduces overall risk

title: Enterprise Risk Management (Continued)
body: Advantages of an ERM program include
Improved risk assessment
Increased risk awareness
An integrated response to the full range of risks
Alignment of the organization’s risk tolerance with its strategies and practices
Fewer operational surprises and losses
Increased competitive advantage
Reduced earnings volatility
Better compliance with corporate governance guidelines
Reduced litigation risk

title: Enterprise Risk Management (Continued)
body: There can (and will) be resistance to implementation of a comprehensive ERM program
Barriers to a successful ERM program include
Rigid organizational culture
Lack of committed leadership
Turf battles between departments over responsibilities
Lack of a formal process
Lack of information
Lack of information sharing and transparency
Technological deficiencies
Lack of commitment to the design and implementation of the program

title: The Enterprise Risk Management Process
body: ERM can be time consuming and costly, but done well can give a company a competitive advantage. Done poorly, can result is disaster or bankruptcy

title: The Risk Management Process

Information collection

Frequency = rate of loss
Severity = cost of loss; usually measured in monetary terms
Develop and select methods for managing risk
Method(s) might need to be developed
Implementation of chosen risk management methods
Monitoring the performance and suitability of the methods on an ongoing basis
Learn by doing and make adjustments as necessary

title: Combined Ratio Calculation: Example 1

Interpretation: Insurers paid out 5% more in claims and expenses than they earned in premiumunderwriting loss (The combined ratio does not take into account investment earnings)

Loss Forecasting
body: The risk manager (or insurer) can predict losses using several different techniques
Probability analysis
Regression analysis
Forecasting based on loss distribution
Of course, there is no guarantee that losses will follow past loss trends

title: Loss Forecasting (Continued)
body: Probability analysis: the risk manager can assign probabilities to individual and joint events.
The probability of an event is equal to the number of events likely to occur (X) divided by the number of exposure units (N)

title: Loss Forecasting (Continued)
body: Two events are considered independent events if the occurrence of one event does not affect the occurrence of the other event.
Example: Independent Events
Suppose the probability of a fire at plant A is 4% and the probability of a fire at plant B is 5%. Then
Note that the probability of a fire in one plant does not affect the probability of fire in the other

title: Loss Forecasting (Continued)
body: Two events are considered dependent events if the occurrence of one event affects the occurrence of the other.
Suppose the probability of a fire at the second plant, given that the first plant has a fire (which has a 3% chance of experiencing a fire), is 40%. Then
Example: Dependent Events

title: Loss Forecasting (Continued)
body: Two events are mutually exclusive if the occurrence of one event precludes the occurrence of the second event.
Example: A building is destroyed by fire it cannot also be destroyed by flood
Suppose the probability a plant is destroyed by a fire is 2% and the probability a plant is destroyed by a flood is 1%. Example: Mutually Exclusive Events
POINT: Mutually exclusive risks are additive
In this case 3% = probability that the plant is destroyed by fire or flood

title: Loss Forecasting (Continued)
body: Regression analysis characterizes the relationship between two or more variables and then uses this characterization to predict values of a variable
For example, the number of physical damage claims for a fleet of vehicles is a function of the size of the fleet and the number of miles driven each year

Loss Forecasting (Continued)
body: A loss distribution is a probability distribution of losses that could occur
Useful for forecasting if the history of losses tends to follow a specified distribution, and the sample size is large
The risk manager needs to know the parameters of the loss distribution, such as the mean and standard deviation
The normal distribution is widely used for loss forecasting

body: Risk managers use the time value of money when analyzing insurance bids or making risk-control investment decisions
Capital budgeting is a method for determining which capital investment projects a company should undertake.
The net present value (NPV) is the sum of the present values of the future cash flows minus the cost of the project
The internal rate of return (IRR) on a project is the average annual rate of return provided by investing in the project

body: A risk management information system (RMIS) is a computerized database that permits the risk manager to store, update, and analyze risk management data

Insurers’ use of credit scoring is an example of predictive analytics
A risk map is a grid detailing the potential frequency and severity of risks faced by the organization
Each risk must be analyzed before placing it on the map

body: Value at risk (VAR) analysis involves calculating the worst probable loss likely to occur in a given time period under regular market conditions at some level of confidence
The VAR is determined using historical data or running a computer simulation
Often applied to a portfolio of assets
Can be used to evaluate the solvency of insurers

title: Types of Private Insurers (Continued)
body: A stock insurer is a corporation owned by stockholders
Objective: earn profit for stockholders by increasing the value of stock and paying dividends
Stockholders elect board of directors
Stockholders bear all losses
Insurer cannot issue an assessable policy
Examples: AIG, Travelers, Progressive, Allstate, MetLife
(Many are foreign: Allianz, Swiss Re, Munich Re, Scor, Ping An, Tokio Marine)

title: Types of Private Insurers (Continued)
body: A mutual insurer is a corporation owned by the policyowners
Policyowners elect board of directors, who have effective management
Policyholders may receive dividends or rate reductions
Examples: State Farm, Auto-Owners, Liberty Mutual, Amica

body: 88% of mutuals have “Strongest” or “Very Strong” balance sheet strength compared to 76% of stock cos. None of the mutual companies are assessed as “Weak” or “Very Weak”

title: Types of Private Insurers (Continued)
body: There are three main types of mutual insurers
An advance premium mutual is owned by the policyowners; there are no stockholders, and the insurer does not issue assessable policies
An assessment mutual has the right to assess policyowners an additional amount if the insurer’s financial operations are unfavorable
A fraternal insurer is a mutual insurer that provides life and health insurance to members of a social or religious organization

title: Types of Private Insurers (Continued)
body: The corporate structure of mutual insurers is changing due to
An increase in company mergers
Demutualization, whereby a mutual company is converted into a stock insurer
The creation of mutual holding companies
A holding company is a company that directly or indirectly controls an authorized insurer
Utilization of a mutual holding company structure with stock subsidiaries may allow more flexibility in terms of raising capital

title: Types of Private Insurers (Continued)
body: Lloyd’s of London is not an insurer, but a society of members who underwrite insurance in syndicates
Membership includes corporations, individual members (called Names), and limited partnerships
New individual members now have limited legal liability
Corporations with limited legal liability and limited liability partnerships can also join Lloyd’s of London
Members must meet stringent financial requirements
Lloyd’s is licensed only in a small number of jurisdictions in the U.S.
Group of RMI students every year goes to London. Lloyd’s is a highlight of that visit.

title: Types of Private Insurers (Continued)
body: A reciprocal exchange can be defined as an unincorporated organization in which insurance is exchanged among the members (called subscribers)
Insurance is exchanged among the members; each member of the reciprocal insures the other members
It is managed by an attorney-in-fact
Most reciprocals are relatively small and specialize in a limited number of lines of insurance, but some are larger…
Example: USAA

title: Types of Private Insurers (Continued)
body: Blue Cross and Blue Shield Plans are generally organized as nonprofit, community oriented plans
Blue Cross plans provide coverage for hospital services
Blue Shield plans provide coverage for physicians’ and surgeons’ fees
Most plans have merged into one entity
Many sponsor HMOs and PPOs (managed care entities)
Some plans have converted to a for-profit status to raise capital and become more competitive

title: Types of Private Insurers (Continued)
body: A Health Maintenance Organization (HMO) provides comprehensive health care services to its members
Broad health care services are provided for a fixed prepaid fee (e.g., annual per capital amount paid to provider no matter how much or how little service is provided)
Cost control is emphasized
Choice of health care providers may be restricted
Less costly forms of treatment are often provided

title: Types of Private Insurers (Continued)
body: A captive insurer is an insurer owned by a parent firm for the purposes of insuring the parent firm’s loss exposures
A single parent, or pure, captive is an insurer owned by one parent
An association captive is owned by several parents
Savings Bank Life Insurance refers to life insurance that is sold by mutual savings banks, over the phone or through Web sites

title: Distribution: Agents and Brokers
body: An agent is someone who legally represents the principal (i.e., insurance co.) and has the authority to act on the principal's behalf
Authority may be
Expressed
Implied
Apparent
The principal (the insurer) is legally responsible for all acts of an agent when the agent is acting within the scope of authority
Insurance agents typically represent
A single insurer (known as a captive or exclusive agent), or
Multiple insurers (independent agent)

title: Agents and Brokers (Continued)
body: A property and casualty agent has the power to bind the insurer (i.e., put the contract in force)
A binder provides temporary insurance until the policy is actually written
A life insurance agent normally does not have the authority to bind the insurer
The applicant for life insurance must be approved by the insurer before the insurance becomes effective

title: Agents and Brokers (Continued)
body: A broker is someone who legally represents the insured, and
solicits applications and attempts to place coverage with an appropriate insurer
is paid a commission from the insurers where the business is placed
does not have the authority to bind the insurer
A surplus lines broker is licensed to place business with a nonadmitted insurer (i.e., an insurer not approved by the state)
Surplus lines refer to any type of insurance for which there is no available market within the state, and coverage must be placed with a nonadmitted insurer
Surplus lines insurers typically insurer harder-to-place risks (i.e., riskier than average)

title: Life Insurance Marketing
body: The majority of life insurance policies and annuities sold today are through personal selling distribution systems
Commissioned agents solicit and sell life insurance products to prospective insureds, as well as employee benefits to businesses
Career, or affiliated, agents are full-time agents who usually represent one insurer and are paid on a commission basis.
In a multiple line exclusive agency system, agents who sell primarily property and casualty insurance also sell individual life and health insurance products.

title: Life Insurance Marketing Systems
body: Independent property and casualty agents are independent contractors who represent several insurers and sell primarily property and casualty insurance but also sell life insurance
A personal-producing general agent (PPGA) is an independent agent who places substantial amounts of business with one insurer and has a special financial arrangement with that insurer
Brokers (in the life insurance context) are independent agents who do not have an exclusive contract with any single insurer

title: Life Insurance Marketing Systems (Cont’d)
body: Many insurers today use commercial banks and other financial institutions as a distribution system
Note: The bank does not assume insurance risk; The bank earns a commission from the sale. All of the risk is held by the insurer.
A direct response system is a marketing system used by life and p/c insurers in which insurance products are sold directly to consumers without a face-to-face meeting with an agent
Includes online sales (for one company or many (aggregator)) or via mail solicitations, television/radio, etc.
Acquisition costs can be held down, but complex products are difficult to sell this way

Worksite marketing (group and supplemental policies)

title: Property and Casualty Insurance Marketing
body: The independent agency is a business firm that usually represents several unrelated insurers
Agents are paid a commission based on the amount of business produced, which vary by the line of insurance, as well as how profitable that business is
The agency (not the insurer) owns the expirations (i.e., renewal rights) to the business; it may bill the policyholders and collect premiums, but most insurers use direct billing
The value of an agency (were it to be sold) is usually a multiple of its annual billings (e.g., 1x)
Agents may be authorized to adjust small claims and may provide loss control services to their insureds

title: Property and Casualty Insurance Marketing (Continued)
body: Under the exclusive agency system, the agent represents only one insurer or group of insurers under common ownership (e.g., State Farm, Allstate)
Exclusive Agents (sometimes called “Captive Agents”) do not usually own the “expirations” or “renewal rights” to the policies
Agents are generally paid a lower commission rate on renewal business than on new business (incentive is to grow by getting new customers)
Exclusive agency insurers provide strong support services to new agents
Most exclusive/captive agents today are independent contractors (not company employees, even though they sell the products of only one insurer)

title: Marketing Systems in Property and Liability Insurance
body: A direct writer is an insurer in which the salesperson is an employee of the insurer, not an independent contractor.
Employees are usually compensated on a “salary plus” arrangement
Less common today with most being independent contractors
A direct response insurer sells directly to the consumer online, television/radio, telephone or some other media (e.g., Geico)
Aggregator: On-line portal selling insurance (or at least generating quotes) from multiple insurers (e.g., insure.com, thezebra.com)
Many property and casualty insurers use multiple distribution systems (e.g., American Family)
NOTE: Distribution of insurance is a big issue in the industry, with various channels both competing and complementing one another.

title: Group Insurance Marketing
body: Many insurers use group marketing methods to sell individual insurance policies to
Employer groups
Labor unions
Trade associations (e.g., AARP & The Hartford) = Affinity Groups
Products are sold through group representatives, employees who receive a salary and incentive payments based on sales.
Some property and liability insurers use mass merchandising plans to market their insurance
Employees typically pay for insurance by payroll deduction

body: Underwriting refers to the process of selecting, classifying, and pricing applicants for insurance
A statement of underwriting policy establishes policies that are consistent with the company’s objectives
The underwriting policy is stated in an underwriting guide, which specifies
Acceptable, borderline, and prohibited classes of business

title: Underwriting Principles
body: The basic principles of underwriting include
Attain an underwriting profit (combined ratio <100)
Select prospective insureds according to the company’s underwriting standards
Reduces adverse selection against the insurer

Adverse selection is the tendency of people with a higher-than-average chance of loss to seek insurance at standard rates. If not controlled by underwriting, this will result in higher-than-expected loss levels.

Note that being the lowest cost insurer is NOT an underwriting principle—if you are always the lowest cost provider, likely means you’ll be adversely selected against (i.e., your product is underpriced)
Provide equity among the policyholders
One group of policyholders should not unduly subsidize another group

title: Steps in Underwriting Process
body: Underwriting starts with the agent (or online)
Information for underwriting comes from
The application
The agent’s report
An inspection report
Physical inspection
Loss reports
Credit information
Driving records
Information from data vendors
A physical examination and attending physician’s report (Life ins.)
MIB (Medical Information Bureau) report (for life insurance)
Increasing number of independent data vendors

title: Steps in Underwriting
body: After reviewing the information, the underwriter can
Accept the application and recommend that the policy be issued
Accept the application subject to restrictions or modifications
Reject the application
Many insurers now use computerized underwriting for certain personal lines of insurance that can be standardized
Underwriters today have much more information available to them than in the past
Consequently, insurers are able to better understand and price risk and therefore underwrite a wider array of risks

title: Other Underwriting Considerations
body: Other factors considered in underwriting include
Rate adequacy
Availability of reinsurance (insurance for insurance cos.)
Whether a policy can or should be cancelled or renewed

title: Production
body: Production refers to the sales and marketing activities of insurers
Agents and brokers are often referred to as producers
Life insurers have an agency or sales department
Property and liability insurers have marketing departments
The marketing of insurance has been characterized by a trend toward professionalism
An agent should be a competent professional with a high degree of technical knowledge in a particular area of insurance and who also places the needs of his or her clients first
Agents are required to be licensed by the state(s) in which they operate and to maintain that license by earning continuing education credits

title: Professional Certifications
body: Several organizations have developed professional designation programs for insurance personnel
The American College: CLU, ChFC
The American Institute for Chartered Property and Casualty Underwriters: CPCU, ARM, ARe
Certified Financial Planner Board of Standards, Inc.: CFP
National Alliance for Insurance Education & Research: CIC
Actuaries: ACAS/FCAS (Associate/Fellow Casualty Actuarial Society)

title: Claim Settlement
body: The objectives of claims settlement include
Verification of a covered loss
Fair and prompt payment of claims
Provide personal assistance to the insured
Laws prohibit unfair claims practices, such as
Refusing to pay claims without conducting a reasonable investigation
Not attempting to provide prompt, fair, and equitable settlements
Offering lower settlements to compel insureds to institute lawsuits to recover amounts due

title: Types of Claims Adjustors
body: Major types of claims adjustors include
An insurance agent often has authority to settle small first-party claims up to some limit
A staff claims representative is usually a salaried employee who will investigate a claim, determine the amount of loss, and arrange for payment.
An independent adjustor is an organization or individual that adjusts claims for a fee
A public adjustor represents the insured and is paid a fee based on the amount of the claim settlement. Usually only involved if there’s a material disagreement on the amount that should be paid

title: Steps in Claim Settlement
body: The claim process begins with a notice of loss, typically immediately or as soon as possible after a loss has occurred.
Next, the claim is investigated
An adjustor must determine that a covered loss has occurred and determine the amount of the loss
The adjustor may require a proof of loss before the claim is paid
The adjustor decides if the claim should be paid or denied
Policy provisions address how disputes may be resolved
NOTE: Some very simple, low dollar claims are adjusted with the insured taking photos and sending to the insurer. Some AI technologies automate the whole process.

title: Steps in Claim Settlement
body: (Adjustor)
In some cases, the insurer may be entitled to recover amount paid through a process called “subrogation”

title: Reinsurance
body: Reinsurance: Easiest way to think about reinsurance is that it is insurance for insurance companies!
It is an arrangement by which the primary insurer that initially writes the insurance transfers to another insurer part or all of the potential losses associated with such insurance
The primary insurer is known as the ceding company (e.g., Allstate)
The insurer that accepts the insurance from the ceding company is the reinsurer (e.g., Swiss Re)
The retention limit is the amount of insurance retained by the ceding company
The amount of insurance ceded to the reinsurer is known as a cession
Retrocession is when a reinsurer insures part or all of a risk with yet another insurer (this process can go on indefinitely)

title: Benefits of Reinsurance
body: Reinsurance: Insurance for insurance companies
Allows insurers to reduce underwriting risk (i.e., the risk that claims costs will exceed expectations)
Stabilizes financial performance, profits
Can reduce ceding insurer’s “unearned premium reserve”
Purchase of reinsurance effectively substitutes for capital
Allows insurer to hold less capital w/o increasing insolvency risk
How? Reinsurer is assuming much of the “tail” risk for catastrophic losses
Losses that exceed a certain magnitude
Accumulation of losses that exceed a certain threshold

title: Types of Reinsurance
body: Treaty Reinsurance: Business that the primary (ceding) insurer agreed to cede to the reinsurer
Usually a block of business of some sort (e.g., FL homes)
Reinsurer agrees to share in losses of all risks in this block
Facultative Reinsurance: Case-by-case reinsurance
Used when the ceding company (primary insurer) receives an application for business that may exceed its “retention limit”
Insurer and reinsurer negotiate a price for the reinsurer to assume some of the risk
Used for more expensive situations (e.g., factory, condo complex, mall)

title: Insured Catastrophe Losses
body: Recent Years Have Been Very Costly for US Insurers and Global Reinsurers

title: Economic Losses from Natural Disasters
body: *Not adjusted for inflation.
Costliest Atlantic Hurricanes*
US Billion-Dollar Weather Disaster Events, 1980 – May 2021

Disaster Count

title: Origins of Pricing Pressure Arising from (Near) Record CAT Activity
body: *Some programs above and below this range.
**Higher end of range applies to loss-affected accounts.
Non-Loss Affected: +5%
Loss-Affected: +10% to +15%**
Retrocessional Reinsurance markets (reinsurance for reinsurance companies) are pushing pressure through the reinsurance markets and into both commercial and personal lines
Insurance for reinsurance companies

title: Types of Reinsurance Agreements
body: There are two principal forms of reinsurance
Facultative reinsurance is an optional, case-by-case method that is used when the ceding company receives an application for insurance that exceeds its retention limit
Often used when the primary insurer has an application for a large amount of insurance
Example: Manufacturing facility, large condo complex, expensive coastal structures
Treaty reinsurance means the primary insurer has agreed to cede insurance to the reinsurer, and the reinsurer has agreed to accept the business
All business that falls within the scope of the agreement is automatically reinsured according to the terms of the treaty
Example: all homes underwritten in SC below a certain value

title: Methods for Sharing Losses
body: There are two basic methods for sharing losses in reinsurance agreements
Under the Pro rata method, the ceding company and reinsurer agree to share losses and premiums based on some proportion
Under the Excess (of loss) or XoL method, the reinsurer pays only when covered losses exceed a certain level

title: Methods for Sharing Losses
body: Under a quota-share treaty (type of pro rata arrangement) the ceding insurer and the reinsurer agree to share premiums and losses based on some proportion
Example: Assume that Apex Fire Insurance and Geneva Re enter into a quota-share arrangement by which losses and premiums are shared 50-50
If a $100,000 loss occurs, Apex Fire pays $100,000 to the insured (i.e., policyholders) but is reimbursed by Geneva Re for $50,000
NOTE: All of this is invisible to the policyholder. The primary insurer adjusts and pays all claims irrespective of the reinsurance arrangements in place.

title: Methods for Sharing Losses
body: Under a surplus-share treaty (another type of pro rata arrangement) the reinsurer agrees to accept insurance in excess of the ceding insurer’s retention limit, up to some maximum amount

Example: Assume that Apex Fire Insurance has a retention limit of $200,000 (called a “line”) for a single policy, and that four lines, or $800,000, are ceded to Geneva Re (so total underwriting “capacity” is $1 million). Assume that a $500,000 property insurance policy is issued. Apex Fire takes the first $200,000 of insurance, or two-fifths, and Geneva Re takes the remaining $300,000, or three-fifths.

title: Methods for Sharing Losses
body: If a $5,000 loss occurs
Apex Fire — $200,000 (1 line)
Geneva Re — $800,000 (4 lines)
Total Underwriting Capacity — $1,000,000
$500,000 policy issued
$5000 loss occurs
Apex Fire — $2000 (2/5)
Geneva Re — $3000 (3/5)
Ex. 2: Suppose the policy issued was for $600,000. What proportion of the loss does Apex retain? What proportion will be paid by Geneva Re?

title: Methods for Sharing Losses
body: An excess-of-loss treaty (XoL) is designed for protection against a catastrophic loss
A treaty can be written to cover a single exposure (e.g., expensive building), a single occurrence (e.g., hurricane), or excess losses

Example: Apex Fire Insurance wants protection for all windstorm losses in excess of $1 million. Assume Apex enters into an excess-of-loss arrangement with Franklin Re to cover single occurrences during a specified time period. Franklin Re agrees to pay all losses exceeding $1 million but only to a maximum of $10 million.Ex. 1: If a $5 million hurricane loss occurs, Franklin Re would pay $4 million (Apex would pay $1 million).
Ex. 2: If a $12 million loss occurred, Apex would pay the first $1 million, Franklin the next $9 million…and Apex pays the final $2 million (since it exhausted its reinsurance)

title: Methods for Sharing Losses
body: A reinsurance pool is an organization of insurers that underwrites insurance on a joint basis
Reinsurance pools work in two ways
Each pool member agrees to pay a certain percentage of every loss.
Each pool member pays for his or her share of losses below a certain amount; losses exceeding that amount are then shared by all members in the pool.

title: Alternatives to Traditional Reinsurance
body: Some insurers use the capital markets as an alternative to traditional reinsurance
Securitization of risk means that an insurable risk is transferred to the capital markets through the creation of a financial instrument, such as a catastrophe bond or futures contract
Catastrophe bonds are corporate bonds that permit the issuer of the bond to skip or reduce the interest payments if a catastrophic loss occurs
Catastrophe bonds are growing in importance and are now considered by many to be a standard supplement to traditional reinsurance.

title: Structure of a Catastrophe Bond
body: Diagram of how a Cat Bond works
Insurer or reinsurer (cedent)
“Special Purpose Vehicle” issues bond
Institutional investors or other (re)insurers (receive interest and return of principal)

title: Establishment and Functioning of a Catastrophe Bond
body: In order to issue a catastrophe bond, the sponsor must first set up an entity known as a special purpose vehicle (SPV). The SPV has the legal authority to act as an insurer and is bankruptcy remote. As such, the SPV protects both parties to the transaction from each other’s credit default risk.
The sponsor enters into a re/insurance contract with the SPV
The SPV issues (sells) the catastrophe bond to investors

The SPV invests the investors’ principal in highly-rated and liquid collateral securities, such as U.S. Treasury Bills. The collateral is safely held in a trust account yields a steady stream of variable interest rate payments that are transferred to the investors. Hence, catastrophe bonds are essentially floating rate notes that exhibit minimal interest rate risk. In addition to the floating returns, catastrophe bonds compensate investors for bearing the disaster risk with premium payments from the sponsor.

If a pre-defined trigger event occurs during the term of the bond, all or part of the principal is transferred to the sponsor, leading to a full or partial loss for investors. If the bond expires without the trigger event occurring, the principal is returned to the investors.

title: Estimating the Risk to Investors in Catastrophe Bonds
body: Modeling: Issuance of a catastrophe bond typically requires engaging a specialized modeling firm to quantify the catastrophe risk (e.g., RMS).
The risk modeler will estimate the probability that the conditions for the payout would be met over the term of the bond (probability of first loss)

The modeling firm also provides an estimate of the expected loss for investors. To this end, it uses its complex scientific models of natural catastrophes in combination with data on insured properties and policy conditions.

title: How Are Catastrophe Bond Payouts “Triggered”? 4 Trigger Types
body: Indemnity Trigger: Payout based on actual losses sustained by the sponsor. As such, they represent a complete “hedge” against disaster risk
Industry Loss Trigger: For payout to sponsor to occur, aggregate losses to the industry as a whole must exceed a predetermined value

Parametric Trigger: Payout is contingent upon a measurable characteristic of the covered catastrophe (e.g., hurricane windspeed, earthquake marnitude) to exceed a predetermined value at a predefined set of locations (measurement stations)
Modeled Loss Trigger: Similar to parametric trigger, but relies on 3rd-party modeler to estimate projected loss to sponsor

title: Trigger Types in the Catastrophe Bond Market
body: Indemnity triggers account for 70% of the CAT bond capital outstanding
Type of Risk Typically Covered
Wind (e.g., US Hurricane, European windstorms, Japan Typhoon)
Earthquake (e.g., in US, Japan, Mexico, Turkey or elsewhere

title: Investments
body: Insurers are the 2nd or 3rd largest institutional investor in the world—investing trillions of dollars globally
Because premiums are paid in advance, they can be invested until needed to pay claims and expenses
Investment earnings are the 2nd largest source of revenue for insurers, behind premiums
Investment income is extremely important in reducing the cost of insurance to policyowners and offsetting unfavorable underwriting experience
Life insurance contracts are long-term; thus, safety of principal is a primary consideration
In contrast to life insurance, property insurance contracts are short-term in nature, and claim payments can vary widely depending on catastrophic losses, inflation, medical costs, etc

title: Exhibit 6.2 Growth of Life Insurer Assets
INVESTMENTS: THE NEW REALITY
body: Investment Performance is a Key Driver of Profitability

title: Other Insurance Company Functions
body: Information Systems are extremely important in the daily operations of insurers.
Computers are widely used in many areas, including policy processing, simulation studies, market analysis, and policyholder services.
The Accounting department prepares financial statements and develops budgets
In the Legal department, attorneys are used in advanced underwriting and estate planning
Property and liability insurers also provide many loss control services

title: Financial Statements of Property and Casualty Insurers
body: A balance sheet is a summary of what a company owns (assets) and what it owes (liabilities), and the difference between total assets and total liabilities (owners’ equity)
Total Assets = Total Liabilities + Owners’ Equity

title: Financial Statements of Property and Casualty Insurers (Continued)
body: The primary assets for an insurance company are financial assets (bonds, stocks, etc.)
Primarily liabilities are Reserves
Insurers’ liabilities include required reserves
A loss reserve is an estimated amount for
Claims reported and adjusted, but not yet paid
Claims reported and filed, but not yet adjusted
Even claims incurred but not yet reported to the company!

title: Financial Statements of Property and Casualty Insurers (Continued)
body: Case reserves are loss reserves that are established for each individual claim (i.e., a “case”)
Methods for determining case reserves include
The judgment method: a claim reserve is established for each individual claim
The average value method: an average value is assigned to each claim
E.g., Avg. auto property damage claim is $5,000
The tabular method: loss reserves are determined for certain claims (e.g., disability claims) for which the amounts paid depend on data derived from mortality, morbidity, and remarriage tables

For example—Someone who is paralyzed due to a work-related injury may never work again. The size of the benefit will be based on age at the time of injury (helps determine expected life span), nature of the injury, etc.

title: Financial Statements of Property and Casualty Insurers (Continued)
body: The unearned premium reserve is a liability item that represents the unearned portion of gross premiums on all outstanding policies at the time of valuation (e.g., if policyholder pays $1,200 on Jan. 1, the insurer “earns” that premium at the pace of $100 per month, not all at once. For examply as of Jan. 31, the insurer will have earned $100 and the remaining $1,100 are Unearned Premiums)
Largest of all reserve categories
Its purpose is to pay for losses that occur during the policy period
It is also needed so that refunds can be paid to policyholders that cancel their coverage
It also serves as the basis for determining the amount that must be paid to a reinsurer for carrying reinsured polices
The annual pro rata method is one method of calculating the reserve

title: Financial Statements of Property and Casualty Insurers (Continued)
body: Policyholders’ surplus is the difference between an insurance company’s assets and liabilities: A-L = PHS
The stronger a company’s surplus position, the greater is the security for its policyholders
The level of surplus is an important determinant of the amount of new business that an insurance company can write

title: Financial Statements of Property and Casualty Insurers (Continued)
body: The income and expense statement summarizes revenues and expenses paid over a specified period of time
The two principal sources of revenue for an insurance company are
premiums, and
investment income
Earned premiums are those premiums for which the service for which the premiums were paid (insurance protection) has been rendered
Expenses include the cost of adjusting claims, paying the insured losses that occurred, commissions to agents, premium taxes, and general insurance expenses

title: Measuring Profit or Loss (Continued)
body: The investment income ratio compares net investment income to earned premiums
From Income Statement: $18M/$205M = 8.8%
The overall operating ratio is equal to the combined ratio minus the investment income ratio
Operating Ratio = 92.2% – 8.8% = 83.4%

title: Financial Statements of Property and Casualty Insurers (Continued)
body: The primary assets for an insurance company are financial assets
Primarily liabilities are Reserves
Insurers’ liabilities include required reserves
A loss reserve is an estimated amount for
Claims reported and adjusted, but not yet paid
Claims reported and filed, but not yet adjusted
Claims incurred but not yet reported to the company

title: Financial Statements of Life Insurers
body: The Balance Sheet

The assets of a life insurer have a longer duration, on average, than those of property and casualty insurers because liabilities are longer in duration (sell lots of products designed to protect against mortality risk and longevity risk—which are long-run risks)
Because many life insurance policies have a savings element, life insurers keep an interest-bearing asset called “contract loans” or “policy loans”
A life insurance company may have separate accounts for assets backing interest-sensitive products, such as variable annuities

title: Financial Statements of Life Insurers (Continued)
body: Policy Reserves are a liability item on the balance sheet that must be offset by assets equal to that amount
State laws specify the minimum basis for calculating policy reserves
Reserve for Amounts Held on Deposit is a liability that represents funds that are owed to policyholders and to beneficiaries
Asset Valuation Reserve is a statutory account designed to absorb asset value fluctuations not caused by changing interest rates

title: Financial Statements of Life Insurers (Continued)
body: Policyholders’ surplus (assets – liabilities) is less volatile in the life insurance industry than in the property and casualty insurance industry (not subject to the same type of huge catastrophe losses p/c insurers experience)
Benefit payments, including death benefits paid to beneficiaries and annuity benefits paid to annuitants, are the life insurer’s major expense
A life insurer’s net gain from operations equals total revenues less total expenses, policyowner dividends, and federal income taxes

title: Measuring the Performance of Life Insurers
body: A number of measures can be used to gauge the performance of life insurers
Pre-tax or after-tax net income vs. total assets (i.e., Return on Assest or ROA)
Rate of return on policyowners’ surplus (like and ROE calculation)

title: Ratemaking: In Property and Casualty Insurance
body: State Laws Require
Guiding Principal: “Rates should not be inadequate, excessive or unfairly discriminatory”
Rates should be adequate for paying all losses and expenses (and to earn a reasonable profit)
Rates should not be excessive, such that policyholders are paying more than the actual value of their protection
Rates must not be unfairly discriminatory; exposures that are similar with respect to losses and expenses should not be charged significantly different rates

title: Ratemaking in Property and Casualty Insurance (Continued)
body: Business Rate-Making Objectives Include
Rates should be easy to understand
Rates should be stable over short periods of time
Rates should be responsive over time to changing loss exposures (e.g., more hurricanes) and changing economic conditions (e.g., lower interest rates)
The rating system should encourage loss control activities

title: Basic Ratemaking Definitions
body: A rate is the price per unit of insurance.
An exposure unit is the unit of measurement used in insurance pricing, e.g., an “insured car-year”
The pure premium is the portion of the rate needed to pay losses and loss adjustment expenses (i.e., costs arising directly from the claim)
Loading is the amount that must be added to the pure premium for other expenses, profit, and a margin for contingencies
The gross rate consists of the pure premium and a loading element
The gross premium paid by the insured consists of the gross rate multiplied by the number of exposure units

title: Ratemaking in Property and Casualty Insurance
body: There are three basic rate making methods in property and casualty insurance: Judgment, Class (Manual) and Merit

Judgment Rating means that each exposure is individually evaluated, and the rate is determined largely by the judgment of the underwriter (pure judgment becoming less common in this era of “Big Data”)

Class, or Manual Rating means that exposures with similar characteristics are placed in the same underwriting class, and each is charged the same rate (e.g., 25-year old male drivers in SC with 1 moving violation; masonry constructed home in Columbia between 10 and 20 years old, no claims)
Insurers now calculate rates for many thousands of classes

title: Ratemaking in Property and Casualty Insurance (Continued)
body: Class Rates are determined using two basic methods
Under the pure premium method, the pure premium can be determined by dividing the dollar amount of incurred losses and loss-adjustment expenses by the number of exposure units
Under the loss ratio method, the actual loss ratio is compared with the expected loss ratio, and the rate is adjusted accordingly

title: Ratemaking in Property and Casualty Insurance (Cont’d)
body: Loss Ratio Method for Class Rating Example: Actual loss ratio is compared with the expected loss ratio, and adjusted accordingly
Assume Insurance has incurred losses and loss adjustment expenses of $800,000 and earned premium of $1 million.
Based on the past 10 years of experience, Expected LR = 0.70
How much do rates need to be increased to reflect the new, higher LR?
Calculate % difference between actual and expected
(0.80 – 0.70)/0.70 = 14.3%  Implies rates need to rise by 14.3%

title: Ratemaking in Property and Casualty Insurance (Cont’d)
body: Merit Rating is a rating plan by which class rates are adjusted upward or downward based on individual loss experience. Based on the assumption that loss experience of a particular insured will differ substantially from that of other insureds
3 Types of Merit Rating Plans
Under a schedule rating plan, each exposure is individually rated
E.g., type of construction (Construction: (e.g., masonry vs. wood frame) Occupancy (residential, commercial, vacant), Protection (e.g., fire hydrants nearby, quality of local fire dept.)
Under experience rating, the class or manual rate is adjusted upward or downward based on past loss experience (often the past 3 years)
Under a retrospective rating plan, the insured’s loss experience during the current policy period determines the actual premium paid for that period

title: Ratemaking in Property and Casualty Insurance (Cont’d)
body: Experience Rating Example
Annual premiums = $30,000
Expected loss ratio = 30%
Actual loss ratio over the past few years is 20%
Credibility Factor (C) = .29 (factor based on volume of experience)
Low C means low volume, so including a “credibility factor” will reduce volatility when there’s only a limited amount of historical experience
Premium Change = (Actual – Expected)/Expected x C
Premium should be adjusted to: $30,000 x( 1-0.097) = $27,090

title: Ratemaking in Life Insurance
body: Life insurance actuaries use a mortality table or individual company experience to determine the probability of death at each attained age
Expected future payments are discounted back to the start of the coverage period and summed to determine the “net single premium” or level installment premiums
The annual expected value of death claims equals the probability of death times the amount the insurer must pay if death occurs
More about ratemaking in L/H insurance later in the course

title: Reasons for Insurance Regulation
body: Maintain insurer solvency
Compensate for inadequate consumer knowledge
Ensure reasonable rates
Make insurance available
Ensure claims are paid fairly and expeditiously

title: Financial Strength & Underwriting
body: Cyclical Pattern is P-C Impairment History is Directly Tied to Underwriting, Reserving & Pricing

title: Insolvency of Insurers
body: Insolvency of insurers continues to be an important regulatory concern
Insurers rarely go insolvent
Reasons for insolvencies include
Inadequate rates
Inadequate reserves for claims
Rapid growth and inadequate surplus
Mismanagement and fraud
Bad investments
Problems with affiliates
Overstatement of assets
Catastrophe losses
Failure of reinsurers to pay claims
Litigation environment (e.g., Florida)

title: A.M. Best Rating Scale
body: As a practical matter, it’s very difficult for an insurer to operate unless it has a rating of A- or better. Many businesses will not buy insurance from an insurer with a rating of below A- (and many brokers will not place business with you)
Example: MetLife

title: Insolvency of Insurers (Continued)
body: The principal methods of ensuring solvency are
Minimum capital and surplus requirements
Risk-based capital standards
Reserve requirements
Restrictions on investments
Review of annual financial statements
Field examinations
Early warning system (IRIS ratios = Insurance Regulatory Information System)
Insurers also each year meet with A.M. Best, the primary private ratings agency, to review their rating and “outlook”

title: A History of Insurance and the Rise of Regulation
body: The Roots of Insurance Extend Back Thousands of Years
Formal Regulation Came Much Later

title: In the Beginning…
body: Civilizations Long Ago Discovered the Benefits of Risk Pooling and Risk Transfer

title: Origins of Insurance…and Insurance Regulation
body: The Rise of Long Distance Trade: The Explosion of Risk and Reward
14th Century: Italian city states of Venice, Florence, Genoa and Pisa became global epicenters for trade and are where the earliest written insurance contract originated
The word “policy” is from the Italian “polizza” meaning promise or undertaking
Bruges, Antwerp followed in the 15th century, Amsterdam by 17th century
By 1600 England had become a major trading nation
From Expensive Cargo/Ships Arose Disputes and the Need for Certainty and the Foundations for Insurance Regulation Were Laid
“For whom they insure, it is sweet to them to take the monies; but when disaster comes, it is otherwise, and each man draws his rump back and strives not to pay.”
Franceso di Marco Datini, Florentine Merchant, 14th Century, complaining about insurers of his era (Datini left 400 marine insurance policies in his estate when he died)
“For even though I were to live a thousand years, never again would I underwrite insurance.”
Guiglielmo Barberi, 14th Century, lamenting the loss of a bale of cloth and a barrel of furs he had underwritten on a ship that had been plundered by pirates, but had no ability to pay

title: Origins of Insurance…and Insurance Regulation
body: London and the Dawn of Insurance Regulation
Italian forms of marine insurance contracts were used in London since at least the 15th century
London merchants frequently acted as underwriters
Contracts were negotiated by commodity brokers
Notaries drafted/delivered policies and kept registers of policies written
Chamber of Assurances established in 1576 and until 1690 all policies had to be registered in its office in the Royal Exchange
1601: Francis Bacon Introduces Bill to Regulate Insurance Policies
Bacon recognized the ubiquity and of utility of insurance contracts which were “tyme out of mynde in usage amonste merchants, both of this realm and of forraine nacyons.”
Led to 1601 Act of Parliament that formally recognized that the benefits of insurance justified legal sanction, with the government willing to enforce insurance contracts and resolve disputes

title: The 8 Stages (Waves) of Insurance Regulation in the United States
body: Regulation in the U.S. Has Been Characterized by Periodic Pulses of Activity

body: Number of Recessions Since 1860
Longevity Requires an Insurer to Overcome Extreme Economic Adversity of Every Sort
Number of Years in Operation

body: George Washington
