title: Origins of Insurance…and Insurance Regulation
body: ANSWER: Ben Franklin
The Philadelphia Contributionship for the Insurance of Houses from Loss by Fire The Contributorship.
Was organized by Benjamin Franklin in 1752 and is the oldest mutual fire insurance company in the United States (website: 1752.com)

Pictured: Office at 212 S. 4th Street between Walnut and Locus Streets in the Society Hill neighborhood of Philadelphia was built in 1835-36 and was designed by Thomas U. Walter in the Greek Revival style, with Corinthian columns. The portico was replaced in 1866 by Collins and Autenreith who also expanded the living quarters on the top two floors by the addition of a mansard roof. A marble cornice between the 3rd and 4th floors was also added.

title: The Supreme Court Reinforces (Establishes) the Primacy of State Regulation of Insurance
body: Paul vs. Virginia (1869)
Since its mid-18th century origins in the US, insurance had been regulated under the general laws governing commerce in the states in which the insurer had been granted a charter/license to operate

As the US economy expanded and insurers (based mostly in the Northeast) sought to expand along with the country, they wanted to avoid the cost and complexity of complying with the many and varied requirements promulgated by the states

Virginia in 1866 enacted legislation requiring a $30,000+ bond be deposited with the state treasurer as a condition of licensure for out-of-state insurers (the agents representing them needed a license as well)
Test Case: Insurers determined to challenge the law asserting that VA’s law interfered with the federal government’s constitutional power to regulate interstate commerce
States opposed since they generated significant revenues from the taxation of premiums
Several NY insurance companies appointed as their agent in VA Samuel D. Paul, a Petersburg, VA, attorney.

title: The Supreme Court Reinforces (Establishes) the Primacy of State Regulation of Insurance
body: Paul vs. Virginia (1869)
Paul applied for a license which was denied because the bond had not been deposited but continued to sell insurance
Paul was indicted, convicted and fined ($50)
Case was eventually appealed to the US Supreme Court which ruled unanimously in VA’s favor
Chief Justice Stephen J. Field delivered the court’s opinion that

“Issuing a policy of insurance is not a transaction of commerce. The policies are simple contracts of indemnity against loss by fire…They are not commodities to be shipped from one state to another, and put up for sale. They are like personal contracts between parties which are completed by their signature and transfer of consideration…The policies do not take effect—are not executed contracts—until delivered by the agent in Virginia, They are, then, local transactions, governed by local law.”
This settled the law on the matter of state vs. federal regulation for the next 75 years (i.e., the Supreme Court had ruled that insurance regulation was the purview of the states)!

title: Out With the Old…In With Dual Regulation
body: 1944 SEUA Supreme Court decision effectively overturned the 1869 Paul v. Virginia decision—after 75 years
State and Federal regulation of insurance were both constitutional
This created an obvious dilemma with no obvious solution
Congress stepped into the void
McCarran-Ferguson Act of 1945
Crafted a partial exemption of the business of insurance from the Sherman, Clayton and FTC Acts to the extent it is regulated by the states
Maintained that federal antitrust laws do apply in cases of boycott, coercion or intimidation
Widely misunderstood by industry critics (including occasionally some members of Congress) as a blanket exemption from antitrust statutes
NAIC’s 1946 All Industry Bill became the model law establishing a framework for regulation in the wake of McCarran-Ferguson
Stringent rate regulation became the norm and by 1948 all states had enacted rate regulatory laws, usually in line with the All Industry Bill
The McCarran-Ferguson Act (1945) states that continued regulation and taxation of the insurance industry by the states are in the public interest
Federal antitrust laws apply to insurance only to the extent that the insurance industry is not regulated by state law

title: Global Financial Crises & Global Systemic Risk: Key Dates
body: Implementation Date — Action
July 2013 — Designation of G-SIIs (annual updates thereafter beginning Nov. 2014)
July 2014 — FSB to make a decision on the G-SII status of, and appropriate risk mitigating measures for major reinsurers
By G-20 Summit 2014 — IAIS to develop backstop capital requirements to apply to all group activities, incl. non-ins. subs.
End 2015 — IAIS to develop HLA requirements that will apply to G-SIIs staring in 2019
January 2019 — G-SIIs to apply HLA requirements

title: Historical Development of Insurance Regulation
body: Insurers were initially subject to few regulatory controls.
State legislatures first granted charters to new insurers; insurance commissions were first created in 1851.

In Paul v. Virginia (1868), the Supreme Court ruled that insurance was not interstate commerce, and that the states rather than the federal government had the right to regulate the insurance industry.

title: Historical Development of Insurance Regulation (Continued)
body: In U.S. v. South-Eastern Underwriters Association (1944) the Court ruled that insurance was interstate commerce when conducted across state lines and was subject to federal antitrust laws
The McCarran-Ferguson Act (1945) states that continued regulation and taxation of the insurance industry by the states are in the public interest
Federal antitrust laws apply to insurance only to the extent that the insurance industry is not regulated by state law

title: Historical Development of Insurance Regulation (Continued)
body: The Financial Modernization Act (1999) changed federal law that earlier prevented banks, insurers, and investment firms from competing outside their core area
State insurance departments regulate insurers
State and federal bank agencies regulate banks
The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regulates the sale of securities
The Federal Reserve has umbrella authority over bank affiliates that engage in underwriting insurance

title: Methods for Regulating Insurers
body: The three principal methods used to regulate insurers are
Legislation: through both state and federal laws
Court Decisions: e.g., interpreting policy provisions
State Insurance Departments
Insurance is regulated primarily by the states in the US (in other countries, insurance is typically regulated by the federal government)

title: 8 Primary Areas of Insurance Regulatory Focus
body: Formation and Licensing of Insurers
Solvency
Investments
Rate Regulation
Sales Practices
Claims Practices
Market Conduct

title: What Areas Are Regulated?
body: All states have requirements for the formation and licensing of insurers
Licensing includes minimum capital and surplus requirements
A domestic insurer is domiciled in the state
A foreign insurer is an out-of-state insurer that is chartered by another state, but licensed to operate in the state
An alien insurer is an insurer that is chartered by a foreign country, but is licensed to operate in the state

title: What Areas Are Regulated?
body: Admitted vs. Non-Admitted Insurers
Admitted Carrier: Approved (and typically licensed) by the state’s insurance Department (i.e., the regulator) to do business in the state
Insurer must comply with all state insurance laws
Insurer participates in state guaranty funds (which protect policyholders in the event of an insolvency by “guarantying” that claims will be paid)
Company is subject to consumer protection laws governing claims handling
Non-Admitted Carrier: Not Approved by the state’s insurance Department to do business in the state
Insurer may not always comply with all state insurance laws
Insurer does not participate in state guaranty fundsmeaning no guaranty claim will paid if insurer becomes insolvent
Consumer has no recourse with state regulators if they feel claim is handled inappropriately
Why Do Business with Non-Admitted Insurer? Coverage not available anywhere else. Such carriers are rated by ratings agencies such as A.M. Best so buyers have some assurance of strength.

title: What Areas Are Regulated? (Continued)
body: Solvency: Insurers are subject to financial regulations designed to maintain solvency
Assets must be (more than) sufficient to offset liabilities
Admitted assets are assets that an insurer can show on its statutory balance sheet in determining its financial condition
States have regulations that address the calculation of reserves
An insurer’s surplus (net worth/capital) position is carefully monitored by state regulators

title: What Areas Are Regulated? (Continued)
body: Insurers must meet certain Risk-Based Capital (RBC) standards
Insurers must hold a certain amount of capital, depending on the riskiness of their investments and insurance operations
An insurer’s RBC depends on asset risk, underwriting risk, interest rate risk, and business risk
A comparison of the company’s total adjusted capital to the amount of required risk-based capital determines whether company or regulatory action is required

title: What Areas Are Regulated? (Continued)
body: The purpose of investment regulations is to prevent insurers from making unsound investments that could threaten the company’s solvency and harm the policyowners
Laws generally place a limit on the proportion of assets in a specific asset category, such as real estate
Many states limit the amount of surplus a participating life insurer can accumulate, rather than pay as dividends

title: What Areas Are Regulated? (Continued)
body: Each insurer must file an annual report with the state insurance department in the states where it does business
The state insurance department assumes control of insurance companies that they determine to be financially impaired
All states have guaranty funds, guaranty laws and guaranty associations that pay the claims of policyowners of insolvent insurers
NOTE: Unlike banks, insurers are not federally insured (e.g., FDIC for banks)
The assessment method is the major method used to raise the necessary funds to pay unpaid claims.

This means that the remaining insurers in the market have to pick up the tab in the form of “assessments” for the obligations of the failed insurer if guaranty fund resources are insufficient. Sometimes insurers can pass the assessment through to consumers, sometimes not.

title: Risk-Based Capital (RBC) Standards
body: Risk-Based Capital Standards: Designed by National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) to reduce the risk of insolvency
RBC: Means that insurers must insurers must hold a certain amount of capital, depending on the riskiness of their investments and insurance operations
An insurer’s RBC depends on asset risk, underwriting risk, interest rate risk, and business risk
A comparison of the company’s total adjusted capital (TAC) to the amount of required risk-based capital (RBC Ratio) determines whether company or regulatory action is required
RBC Ratio = Total Adjusted Capital/RBC
Example: Suppose an insurer has TAC = $22 billion and RBC of $10 billion, then the RBC Ratio = 22/10 = 2.2 or 220% (has 2.2x min level of capital)

title: RBC (Continued)
body: Comparison of Risk Charges

title: RBC (Continued)
body: Comparison of Risk Charges for Different Asset Classes
Increasing Risk
Insurer has to hold 30% of the value of a low-rated asset (e.g., below investment grade bond) but 0% for a US Govt. bond

title: Example: RBC Calculation for Insurance
body: Investment Portfolio RBC Charge Required Capital
Premiums Written
Reserves
If ’s = Total Adjusted Capital is $4,000,000, what is its RBC Ratio?
TAC = $4 million and RBC of $1.61 million, then the RBC Ratio = 4/1.61 = 2.48 or 248% (has 2.48x min level of capital)

title: RBC (Continued)
body: Corrective Action Levels for Insurers
RBC Ratio: 125% and above · Zone: Adequate · Action: None
RBC Ratio: 100% - 124% · Zone: Red Flag · Action: Insurer must conduct trend test
RBC Ratio: 75% - 99% · Zone: Company Action · Action: Insurer must file plan with regulator outlining corrective steps
RBC Ratio: 50% - 74% · Zone: Regulatory Action · Action: Regulator must examine insurer and order corrective steps
RBC Ratio: 35% - 49% · Zone: Authorized Control · Action: Regulator may seize insurer, if necessary
RBC Ratio: Below 35% · Zone: Mandatory Control · Action: Regulator must seize insurer

title: What Areas Are Regulated? (Continued)
body: Policy Forms: State insurance commissioners have the authority to approve or disapprove new policy forms before the contracts are sold to the public
Insurance contracts are technical and complex
Purpose is to protect the public from misleading, deceptive, and unfair provisions
Some regulations getting into excruciating detail (Example: In FL homeowners insurance policies, regulation require that the policy’s flood exclusion by printed in an 18-point or larger font!)

title: Price regulation
body: Some Types of Insurance Are Heavily Price Regulated, Others Not at All

title: Price/Rate Regulation
body: Common Types of Rate Regulation
Effect: Restrict level or change in rates
Prior Approval: regulator must approve rates before they can be put into effect by the insurer
File and Use: Insurer “files” rates with regulators and implements immediately though regulator has authority to require adjustments
Use and File: Insurer implements rates and “files” rating plan with regulator afterward
Competitive (Open Competition/No Filing): Rates are market determined w/o regulator input
Restrictions on Underwriting Criteria
Can also have impact similar to regulating rates

title: Effects of Rate Suppression and Compression
body: Higher rates are unpopular with consumers
Rate Suppression – Regulators force insurers to keep rates below actual costs (e.g., not allowed to charge actuarially justified price for homes on the coast)
Rate Compression - Restrict differences in rates across classifications
Suppression and/or compression lead insurers to not voluntarily offer coverage to some groups (supply of insurance falls)
Which leads to creation of residual market mechanisms (e.g., joint underwriting associations) (JUAs)
Sometimes referred to as “markets of last resort”
While this creates some minimal level of availability, the coverage options are limited, prices are expensive, sometimes state-run

title: What Areas Are Regulated? (Continued)
body: Sales Practices are regulated by the laws concerning the licensing of agents and brokers
All states require agents and brokers to be licensed
All states require agents to obtain continuing education to upgrade their knowledge and skills

title: Some Sales Practices/Issues That Are Regulated? (Continued)
body: Insurance laws prohibit a variety of unfair sales practices, such as misrepresentation, twisting, and rebating
Twisting is the inducement of a policyowner to drop an existing policy and replace it with a new one that provides little or no economic benefit to the client

Rebating is the practice of giving an individual a premium reduction or some other financial advantage not stated in the policy as an inducement to purchase the policy (e.g., even giving someone a free fire extinguisher or smart home monitoring systems could violate these laws in some states)
Insurers seeking to get rebating losses changed (e.g., insurers want to provide policyholders with monitoring devices)
Suitability: Any insurance product sold must be appropriate for the buyer. For example, selling a 20-year term life insurance policy to a 90-year old would not meet the suitability standard.

title: What Areas Are Regulated? (Continued)
body: Claim Practices: State insurance departments typically have a complaint division for handling consumer complaints
Most complaints involve claims
Disagreement over amount of damage is the most common dispute
Timely payment of claim can be an issue
Claim could be complex
Suspicion of fraud
Information is provided to consumers on insurance department websites and in brochures
Taxes: Insurers pay numerous local, state, and federal taxes
Especially “premium taxes” which are often assed at 2% of direct premiums written

title: State versus Federal Regulation
body: Proponents for federal regulation argue that federal regulation
would provide uniformity in state regulations
is more effective in negotiations of international insurance agreements
is more effective in the identification and treatment of systemic risk
Would enable insurers to become more efficient

title: State versus Federal Regulation (Continued)
body: Advantages of state regulation include
Quicker response to local insurance problems
Federal regulation could lead to a dual system of regulation and increase costs
Poor quality of federal regulation, e.g., in the banking industry
Reasonable uniformity of laws can be achieved by the model laws of the NAIC
Greater opportunity for innovation
Unknown consequences of federal regulation

title: State versus Federal Regulation (Continued)
body: Shortcomings of state regulation include
Inadequate protection of consumers
Improvements needed in handling complaints
Inadequate market conduct examinations
Insurance availability

title: Current Problems and Issues in Insurance Regulation
body: Should the McCarran-Ferguson Act be repealed?
Critics of state regulation argue
The insurance industry no longer needs broad antitrust exemption
Federal regulation is needed because of the defects in state regulation.
Counterarguments include
The insurance industry is already competitive.
Small insurers may be harmed.
Insurers may be prevented from developing common coverage forms

title: Modernizing Insurance Regulation (Continued)
body: The Federal Insurance Office (FIO) has the authority to
Monitor all aspects of the insurance industry
Identify gaps in insurance regulation and identify issues that contribute to systemic risk
Assist the FSOC in identifying insurers that could create systemic risk
Represent the federal government in international discussions of insurance regulation
Negotiate international agreements with foreign countries that pertain to insurance regulation.

title: Modernizing Insurance Regulation (Continued)
body: Another approach to insurance regulation is an optional federal charter
Proposals would allow insurers to choose either a federal or state charter.
Proponents argue that national insurers are at a competitive disadvantage under the present system.
Opponents suggest this creates a dual system of insurance regulation which will increase the cost of insurance regulation

title: Areas Regulated: Market Conduct Regulation
body: Market Conduct refers to the marketing practices of insurers and agents that involve interaction with insureds, claimants, or consumers. Regulators have the authority to perform “Market Conduct Exams”
Practices include
Sales of insurance policies
Advertising of insurance products
Underwriting and rating
Collection of premiums
Policy renewals, termination, and changes
Claims settlement

title: Market Conduct Regulation (Continued)
body: Regulators are concerned that certain industry practices may have an adverse effect on policyholders, beneficiaries, claimants and insurance consumers
Concerns include
Sale of unsuitable insurance products
Misrepresentation of coverage
Excessive sales pressure
Rates that are excessive or unfairly discriminatory
Denial of legitimate claims
Improper termination of policies
Regulators have authority to conduct “Market Conduct Exam” which involves a detailed inspection of the insurer’s practices and can result in fines, penalties and other sanctions against the insurer

title: Market Conduct Regulation (Continued)
body: The FIO report makes several recommendations regarding market conduct, including
Every state should participate in the Interstate Insurance Product Regulation Commission (IIPRC) to improve the approval of products
Each state should adopt the Suitability in Annuities Transactions Model Regulation drafted by the NAIC
States should identify regulatory practices that best promote competitive markets for personal lines insurance consumers

title: Insight 8.1: Credit-Based Insurance
body: The credit based insurance score is derived from an applicant’s credit history and is combined with other underwriting factors
Proponents of credit-based insurance argue
There is a high correlation between an applicant’s credit record and future claims experience
Insurance scores permit insurers to accept more high-risk consumers for whom otherwise an appropriate premium could not be determined
Most consumers have good credit scores and benefit from credit scoring

title: Insight 8.1: Credit-Based Insurance (Continued)
body: Critics of credit-based insurance argue
The use of credit data in underwriting or rating discriminates against minorities and other groups
Credit-based insurance scores may penalize consumers unfairly during business recessions
Most states have enacted legislation that regulates the use of credit-based insurance scores

title: Insurance industry pricing
body: 4 Major Components of Insurance Premiums

title: Insurance Pricing
body: For insurer to remain in operation it’s premiums must be sufficient to
Cover expected claim costs and administrative costs
Provide an expected profit to compensate for the cost of obtaining (and retaining) the capital necessary to support the sale of coverage
Actuarially “Fair Premium”: Premium that is just sufficient to cover an insurer’s expected costs, including a fair return to capital
It is the premium that would be charged in a perfectly competitive market

title: 4 Determinants of Fair Premiums
body: 4 Determinants of the Actuarially Fair Premium
Expected Claim Costs
Administrative Costs
Investment Income
Fair Profit Loading

title: Expected Claim Costs & Risk Classification
body: Expected Claim Costs
An insurer must cover it’s expected claim costs
Have to recognize that assessment of risk in the real world is complex and risks need to be carefully analyzed so that they can be assigned to appropriate risk “classes”
“Homogeneous” (same) vs. “Heterogeneous” (different) buyers
Homogenous Buyers of Insurance: Assume that all buyers share the same loss distribution (but that losses are uncorrelated with one another)
Heterogenous Buyers of Insurance: Assume that all buyers can be can be categorized into different loss distributions (but that losses are still uncorrelated with one another)
OBJECTIVE: Develop a rating system that is fair

title: Expected Claim Costs
body: Pure Premium: The premium that just covers expected claim costs
Example
Large number of homogeneous buyers, i.e. each has the same loss distribution
Possible Loss Probability

title: Premium Must Cover Expected Claim Costs
body: To cover claim costs, on average, premiums must equal $500.
if premium = $480, the insurer will lose money, on average (and will have to raise prices or will eventually go out of business)
if premium = $640, the insurer will make profits, on average
But competition will eventually compete away this profit
Conclusion
Actuarially Fair Premium must cover expected claim costs (cannot undercharge or overcharge in a perfectly competitive market)

title: Implications of Heterogeneous Buyers
body: What if there are two groups of buyers with different risk characteristics? (Assume a simple loss distribution where losses are either $0 or $10,000)
One Group (Sunday Drivers)
Possible Loss	Probability
Another Group (Speedy Drivers)

title: Expected Loss Components
body: Group: Sunday Driver · Probability of Loss: 0.05 · Probability of NO Loss: 0.95 · Severity of Loss: $10,000 · Expected Value (Loss): $500
Group: Speedy Driver · Probability of Loss: 0.10 · Probability of NO Loss: 0.90 · Severity of Loss: $10,000 · Expected Value (Loss): $1,000

title: Our initial assumptions are that
body: Equal number of each type (Sunday and Speedy—i.e., low-risk and high-risk)
Losses are Independent
Full Insurance is mandatory (i.e., nobody can opt out or purchase less insurance)
Costless to distinguish Sunday and Speedy drivers

title: Heterogeneous Buyers
body: Initial Scenario
Equal Treatment Insurance Company is the only insurer in the market
Premium for everyone = $750
Does Equal Treatment cover its costs? YES
$750 is the EV of all drivers if we combine both groups ($1000+ $500)/2
BUT: the Speedy Drivers pay less than their expected cost, and the Sunday Drivers pay more than theirs

title: Should we treat these two distributions of drivers differently? Should we charge them different premiums?
Equal-Risk Treatment:Company Perspective
body: Charges $750 to all policyholders
Makes a $250 profit on each Sunday driver
Loses $250 on each Speedy driver
Overall, the company breaks even.

title: New Scenario: Allow Competition
body: If Super Selective Insurance enters the market and assumes Equal Treatment will continue to charge $750, suppose Selective sets price with the objective of earning $50 profit per policy as follows
Premium to Sunday Drivers = $550 (profit = $550-$500)
Premium to Speedy Drivers = $1050 (profit = $1050-$1000)
Note: Profit per policyholder = $50
(it’s charging both groups $50 above expected losses)

title: What Happens to Equal Treatment?
body: BUT: Equal Treatment would experience adverse selection
Adverse Selection: Adverse selection is the tendency of persons with higher-than-average chances of loss to seek insurance at standard (average) rates.
If not controlled by underwriting (screening of applicants), adverse selection results in higher-than-expected loss levels.
Equal would obtain an adverse selection of policyholders -- only the Speedy Drivers will purchase from Equal Treatment
True cost of insuring Speedy Drivers is $1,000. Since Equal Treatment is only charging $750 and Selective is charging $1050.
Thus, Equal Treatment will have to classify or lose money.

title: Key Points
body: Profit Maximization
+ ==> Risk Classification
Competition
Lack of Classification
+ ==> Adverse Selection

title: Implications of Heterogeneous Buyers
body: Key Points
Profit Maximization
+ ==> Risk Classification
Risk Management Alternatives
to Insurance
Lack of Classification
+ ==> Adverse Selection

title: Adverse Selection—Recall
body: Adverse selection is the tendency of persons with higher-than-average chances of loss to seek insurance at standard (average) rates.
If not controlled by underwriting, adverse selection results in higher-than-expected loss levels.

title: Adverse Selection: Examples
body: High-risk drivers tend to seek insurance at preferred or standard rates.
Persons with serious health problems tend to seek health or life insurance at standard rates.
Businesses with a long record of burglary or robbery tend to seek property insurance at standard rates.
NOTE: In all of these cases, insurers have mechanisms in place to determine the true riskiness of potential policyholders
Underwriting is the primary tool to control adverse selection

title: Implications of Heterogeneous Buyers
body: What if it is costly to identify Speedy Drivers?
Go back to initial situation, but suppose that it costs $50 for each Speedy Driver identified (e.g., costs $50 to run DMV records)
Assuming Equal Treatment continues to charge $750, what does Selective charge?
Sunday Drivers? ______
Speedy Drivers? ______
Redo the analysis assuming the cost per Speedy Drivers identified = $300 (i.e., Selective would have to charge Sunday drivers $850)
At some point the marginal cost of gathering and analyzing such information with exceed its benefit, and insurer won’t adopt

TODAY: Costs of identifying and analyzing distinguishing risk characteristics are falling due to better technology (ability to analyze and store more information, more inexpensively, than ever before; Emphasis placed on data analytics, “Big Data,” AI, etc.)

title: Implications of Heterogeneous Buyers
body: Key Point
Profit Maximization Risk Classification
+ ==> if it is
Competition Cost Effective

It may be too expensive to identify some factors necessary for risk classification. “Big Data” and “data analytics” can help with this by making more data that is helpful for underwriting purposes available—in a timely and cost-efficient manner.

title: Is Classification Good for Society?
body: Public Policy Issue
From a societal perspective, is risk classification desirable?
Some argue that risk classification should be restricted when
insurance is mandatory (e.g., auto liability)
classification is based on inherited traits (e.g., gender, genes)
classification is based on location of residence (e.g., auto, property)
classification is based on subjective criteria (e.g., “poor moral risks”)
When link between risk factor and risk isn’t immediately or intuitively obvious (e.g., use of credit information, education, occupation)
What do you think? Should life and health insurers be able to use genetic information to determine premiums?

title: Is Classification Good for Society?
body: Restricting classification will alter insurance prices to certain groups and therefore change behavior
Types of behavior
amount of insurance purchased
amount of risky activity undertaken
loss control activities
Some changes in behavior may be desirable and some undesirable
Examples
Should the unvaccinated be charged more for health insurance?
amount of liability insurance purchased by poor people
smoking
amount of life insurance purchased by people with HIV
What about obesity—should overweight people be charged more for health insurance?
Should people who regularly go to health clubs or maintain a low BMI receive discounts?

title: Is Classification Good for Society?
body: Restricting classification may decrease classification costs
Insurers incur costs in classifying, which increases prices on average. We might be better off not incurring these costs and just charge everyone the same price
BUT…Many of the controversial issues (gender, age, location) have low classification costs

title: Is Classification Good for Society?
body: 4. Limits on classification may increase regulatory costs
Monitoring of insurers to enforce restrictions
Need to impose other costly restrictions on insurers
marketing activities
underwriting activities
Restrictions lead insurers to not offer coverage (at all or less coverage)
Leads to residual market (involuntary market) mechanisms
Leads to additional costs

title: Risk Classification Practices
body: Consumers are classified by various criteria
Class Rate: rate that applies to all consumers in a given classification
Underwriter decides whether a particular consumer will be offered coverage at the class rate
Actuaries determine price within each class based on risk characteristics
Schedule Rating: modification of the rate by the underwriter based on specific characteristics of the consumer (applies mostly to commercial insurance)
Underwriter has flexibility to adjust rate (+/-) to reflect unique characteristics that may affect losses
Loss control programs in a business, lack of fence around a pool at a home, presence of a burglar alarm system, condition of the insured property; Many factors will influence rate.

title: Risk Classification Practices (cont’d)
body: Consumers are classified by various criteria
Experience Rating: refers to the practice of basing rates on past experience
Retrospective Experience Rating (Retro Rating): Adjusting premium based on loss experience during policy period (most commonly used in medium/large businesses to incentivize loss control)
Particularly common in workers compensation insurance (incentive is to reduce workplace injuries and associated costs)

title: Recouping versus Updating Rates
body: Basing rates on past experience is sometimes viewed as controversial
Are insurers
Recouping past losses?
Updating expected losses on future business?
Competition and low switching costs limit the ability of insurers to recoup

title: Return to Determinants of Fair Premiums
body: Summary
Ignoring
administrative costs
investment income
profits
Fair Premium = Expected Claim Costs = Pure Premium

title: 4 Determinants of Fair Premiums
body: 4 Determinants of the Actuarially Fair Premium
Expected Claim Costs
Administrative Costs
Investment Income

title: Investment Income
body: Key Point
Fair premium is reduced to reflect investment income on premiums
Equivalently
Fair Premium = Present Value of Expected Costs
(i.e., we discount the amounts to be paid out in the future by a rate that reflects what can be earned on premiums in the interim)

title: Example to Illustrate Effect of Investment Income
body: Assume
No administrative costs
One year policies, premium received at beginning of year
Certain claim costs = $100 paid at year-end
Undiscounted Claim Cost = $100
To calculate discounted claim cost (i.e., assumes claim paid at end of year), must take into account time value of money
If interest rate is
What if instead $50 is paid out at end of Yr. 1 and $50 at end of Yr. 2?

title: Example to Illustrate Effect of Investment Income
body: Assume
no administrative costs
one year policies, premium received at beginning
certain claim costs = $100 paid according to table below (at end of each period)
Fair Premium
Note that the PVs are very sensitive to the timing of claim payments and to interest rates
The cumulative percentage of losses paid over time is known as the Loss Payout Pattern

title: Loss Payout Patterns for Select Lines of Business
body: Homeowners claims are paid out relatively quickly, meaning that investment income is less of an influence in so-called “short-tailed” lines
WC and Other Liability (e.g. Products Liability) claims are paid out relatively slowly, meaning that investment income has a larger influence in so-called “long-tailed” lines

title: Long vs. Short-Tail Lines
body: Short-Tailed Line: Line (type) of insurance where losses are paid out relative quickly
Investment income will have less of an influence
Examples: homeowners, auto comprehensive coverage (covers weather-related claims to vehicles, theft, vandalism)
Long-Tailed Line: Line (type) of insurance where losses are paid out relative slowly
Investment income will have more of an influence
Some claims may not be “closed” for decades
Examples: workers comp, medical malpractice, products liability, directors and officers, employment practices liability
Bottom Line: All else equal, lower interest raise the price of insurance (esp. for LT lines) (and vice versa)

title: Administrative Expenses
body: Fair Premium must cover administrative costs, such as
marketing
underwriting
loss adjustment
premium taxes
underwriting income taxes
etc.

title: Expense Loadings as a Percentage of Premium
4 Determinants of Fair Premiums
body: 4 Determinants of the Actuarially Fair Premium
Expected Claim Costs
Administrative Costs
Investment Income
Fair Profit Loading

title: Summary of Determinants of Fair Premiums
body: Ignoring profit loading
Fair Premium = PV of Expected Costs
Fair Premium = PV of Pure Premium + PV of Expenses
Note: Analysis to this point has been based on expected values
Now take into consideration the uncertainty associated with operations
Owners of capital need to be compensated for the risk they are taking

title: Effect of Uncertainty: Profit Loading
body: Rationale for Profit Loading
UncertaintyClaim costs could exceed premiums
And investment income could fall short of expectations
Therefore, insolvency is possible
Insurers hold capital not only to pay claims but to reduce the likelihood of insolvency
Thus, capital providers bear the risk associated with insurance operations
Capital providers require compensation for the capital they put at risk

title: Fair Profit Loadings
body: Other Terms for Profit Loading
Risk Loading
Capital Costs
Determinants of Fair Profit Loading
Amount of capital needed to support the coverage
Cost of capital (based on relative risk)
Takes into account how risky the business is relative to the “risk-free” rate of return (typically a US Treasury yield)

The more volatile the business, the higher the cost of capital (e.g., lines of business written on property in areas prone to hurricanes will have a higher profit loading than in areas that are less disaster prone)

title: Summary of Fair Premium Calculation
body: Fair Premium
=	PV of Expected Claim Costs
+ PV of Expected Loss Adjustment Expenses
+ Underwriting Expenses
+	Fair Profit Loading
Note fair premiums depend on both notions of risk highlighted in Chapter 1
Expected losses AND
Unpredictability of losses

title: Numerical Example
body: Loss Dist. = $20,000	with prob. 0.08
$ 0 with prob. 0.90
Find Fair Premium if
policy provides full coverage
Underwriting costs = 20% of pure premium
LAE = 10% of pure premium
fair profit = 5% of pure premium
Premium paid at beginning of the year
claims are paid at end of year
interest rate = 8%

title: Numerical Example
body: Solution
PV of Expected Claims = $3600/1.08 = $3,333
PV of LAE = $360/1.08 = $333 (discount because LAE is part of claim)
Underwriting Costs = 0.2($3,600) = $720 (don’t discount because underwriting expenses are incurred immediately)
Fair Premium = (PV of Expected Claims+ PV of LAE) + UW Costs + Fair Profit

title: Capital shocks and underwriting cycles
body: Both Can Impact Pricing

title: Capital Shocks and Underwriting Cycles
body: Cycles and price increases following capital shocks are difficult to explain with fair premium model
Fair premium implies that prices are forward looking
prices depend on expected future costs
prices depend on uncertainty about future costs
What happened in past is irrelevant

title: Premium Increases Following Capital Shocks
body: One explanation
Capital shock depletes insurer capital (Supply of insurance falls)
Costly to raise new capital
Capital becomes scarce ==> its required return increases
That is, the risk load increases

title: Underwriting Cycles
body: Possible Explanations
Insurers naively extrapolate from recent past
high (low) losses in the past cause insurers to predict that future losses will be high (low)
Problem: Sometimes the future is different from the past
Capital shocks, followed by excessive competition
capital shock reduces capital
premiums increase, which replenishes capital
excess capital develops
premiums decrease

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Benefits of a Strong Capital Position
body: Capital is the life’s blood of insurance companies
The more capital a company has, the less likely it is to become insolvent, all else equal
Benefits of Increasing Capital
Consumer Preference for Strength
Consumers prefer to insure with insurers that are financially strong
Effect is particularly true for commercial (corporate) insurance customers
Risk managers and brokers investigate strength before purchase
Usually rely on rating from ratings agencies like A.M. Best
All else equal, a strong insurer can charge more for its products
Reduce Likelihood of Regulatory Action
If regulators have to take action to stave off a capital shortfall or insolvency, that could be the end of the insurer.

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Types of Ownership and Structure of Private Insurance Companies
body: Stock Insurers: Incorporated insurance companies that are owned by investors who have purchased stock (equity) of the company.
Stockholders elect a board of directors who appoint executive officers to manage the company
If profitable, board can decide to pay dividends to shareholders
Examples: Travelers, Allstate, AIG, Chubb, Hartford Financial, MetLife, Aflac, Unum
Mutual Insurer: An insurer that is owned by its policyholders. There are no stockholders.
Policyholders vote to elect a board of directors who appoint executive officers to manage the company

If profitable, board can decide to pay dividends to policyholders or instead may decide to reduce prices (or limit increases); Dividends are not guaranteed (since performance is uncertain) and in practice relatively few pay dividends
Advance Premium Mutual: Policyholders pay premium in advance (Common)
Assessment Mutual: Have right to assess policyholders if financial results unfavorable (Rare)
Fraternal Insurer: Life/Heath insurer that provides coverage to a social or religious organization (Knights of Columbus, Thrivent Financial)
Mutual Examples: Liberty Mutual, State Farm, New York Life, Nationwide, Amica

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Types of Ownership and Structure of Private Insurance Companies
body: Lloyd’s of London: Lloyd’s is not itself an insurer. Lloyd’s is actually composed of many “syndicates” which are specialist insurers that are members of Lloyd’s.
Lloyd’s is composed of approximately 95 syndicates run by a “managing agent”
Syndicates tend to specialize in marine, aviation, catastrophe and professional liability coverages but also write many unusual types of risks (equine, hole-in-one, terrorism)
Major player in global reinsurance markets
Syndicate Examples: Catlin, Amlin, Beazley, Brit, Hiscox

Reciprocal Exchange: An unincorporated organization in which insurance is exchanged among its members (sometimes called subscribers). In effect, insurance is “exchanged” among the members; each member of the reciprocal insures the other members and is likewise insured by them.
Most write only a few property/casualty lines of insurance and are small, but are some exceptions
Reciprocal Example: USAA

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Types of Ownership and Structure of Private Insurance Companies
body: Blue Cross/Blue Shield: BC/BS plans were historically organized as nonprofit, community-oriented hospital pre-payment plans. Today, they are a mixture of non-profit and for-profit health care providers. The non-profits receive some favorable tax treatment. Some of the for-profit BC/BS companies are publically traded.
Health Maintenance Organizations (HMO): HMOs are organized plans of healthcare that provide comprehensive healthcare to members.
Heavy emphasis on cost controls; restrictions of providers, treatments, pharmaceuticals, etc.

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Types of Ownership and Structure of Private Insurance Companies
body: Captives: An insurer owned by a company (whose business is not insurance) for the purposes of insuring its parent company’s loss exposures. Several types of captives, e.g.
Single-Parent (or Pure) Captive: owned by one parent (e.g., often a corporation)
Association Captive: Multiple parents

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Agents and Brokers
body: Agents and Brokers: Agents and brokers are not insurance companies. Agents and brokers sell insurance through insurance agencies and brokers. They bear no insurance risk.
Agent: Legally represents the insurer.
More commonly works with individuals and small businesses
Broker: Legally represents the insured (i.e., the buyer of the insurance)
More commonly represents modest-to-large businesses

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Costs of Increasing Capital
body: If having more capital is always better than less, why don’t insurers contain to raise/build capital until the risk of insolvency is infinitesimally small (near zero)?
Because capital is costly. Investors want a return a return on their capital
There is a cost of capital and therefore an opportunity cost from the perspective investors (i.e., they could invest their money elsewhere)
Cost of capital for mutual insurers lower than it is for stock companies
Benefits of Increasing Capital
Consumer Preference for Strength
Consumers prefer to insure with insurers that are financially strong
Effect is particularly true for commercial (corporate) insurance customers
Risk managers and brokers investigate strength before purchase
Usually rely on rating from ratings agencies like A.M. Best
All else equal, a strong insurer can charge more for its products
Reduce Likelihood of Regulatory Action

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Terrorism Violates Traditional Requirements for Insurability
body: Requirement: Estimable Frequency · Definition: Insurance requires large number of observations to develop predictive rate-making models (an actuarial concept known as credibility) · Violation: Very few data points Terror modeling still in infancy, untested. Inconsistent assessment of threat

Requirement: Estimable Severity · Definition: Maximum possible/ probable loss must be at least estimable in order to minimize “risk of ruin” (insurer cannot run an unreasonable risk of insolvency though assumption of the risk) · Violation: Potential loss is virtually unbounded. Losses can easily exceed insurer capital resources for paying claims. Extreme risk in workers compensation and statute forbids exclusions.

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Requirement: Diversifiable Risk · Definition: Must be able to spread/distribute risk across large number of risks “Law of Large Numbers” helps makes losses manageable and less volatile · Violation: Losses likely highly concentrated geographically or by industry (e.g., WTC, power plants)
body: Requirement: Random Loss Distribution/Fortuity · Definition: Probability of loss occurring must be purely random and fortuitous Events are individually unpredictable in terms of time, location and magnitude · Violation: Terrorism attacks are planned, coordinated and deliberate acts of destruction Dynamic target shifting from “hardened targets” to “soft targets” Terrorist adjust tactics to circumvent new security measures Actions of US and foreign govts. may affect likelihood, nature and timing of attack
Information Institute

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: The Effect of Premium Loadings
body: Premium Loadings: Addition costs added to the pure premium to account for the insurers
Administrative costs
Capital costs (risk load)
Higher Loading lower demand for coverage
Why is demand for coverage lower after loadings are accounted for?
Because the loadings increase the price of coverage
Econ 101: If the price for something rises, all else equal the quantity demanded will fall
RESULT: Buyers will often NOT fully insure
i.e.: they purchase less than full insurance

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: The Effect of Premium Loadings
body: Recall: If an insurance contract’s premium equals the value of expected claim costs, risk-averse person (business) will likely demand full insurance coverage
But because premiums (almost) always have a positive loading (loading > $0), then demand will be less than full coverage

Bottom Line: Any factor (e.g., administrative costs, profit loadings/capital costs) that increases the price of coverage above the expected value of claims (pure premium) will limit the amount of private insurance coverage.
This is one reason people are chronically underinsured

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: The Effect of Premium Loadings
body: Premium loadings can be high enough as to make insurance for certain types of exposures extremely limited or even not available at all
Exposures with Low Severity (i.e., low cost claims)
Exposures with High Frequency
Correlated Exposures
Exposures with Uncertain Expected Losses (aka “Parameter Uncertainty”)

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Exposures with Low Severity
body: Administrative Costs are “Fixed”
The administrative costs of insuring a $200 bicycle are the same as insuring a $2,000 bicycle.
Exposures with low value will not be insured on an individual basis
Basically not worth it—amount of coverage you receive for the price paid is not a good value

Might be done when low value items are grouped under a larger limit (e.g., under the “contents” limits in home insurance policies where by all “contents” in the home are bundled together thus spreading the fixed costs across many items which collectively have a greater value).
Beware of high prices for warranties on inexpensive products

According to Consumer Reports, the average car repair is $180 but an extended warranty costs ~$1,500 (and must be purchased when car is new; Doesn’t kick in until regular warranty expires—e.g., 5-yr, 50K miles)

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Supply Side Impact of Premium Loadings
body: In addition to reducing the demand for coverage, higher loadings affect supply.
Insurance for some types of exposures (with a high loading) is likely to be extremely limited (or nonexistent)

The administrative costs of underwriting could be so large (e.g., small employers with a history of injuring many workers) or the required risk loading so high that insurers may try to limit exposure to certain markets

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Exposures with High Frequency
body: When the probability of loss is high then this implies that expected claim costs (i.e., EVs) are high
And if there are many claims then administrative costs high (e.g., claims adjuster has to make frequent trips)
This is one reason why insurers will non-renew a policy that has many claims filed on it
Implication: Fair Premium is Close to Potential Loss
If administrative costs are high enough the premium can actually exceed the value of the potential loss (insurance would make no sense in the context)
Demand for insurance in these situations is low.
Premiums are high relative to the potential loss

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Exposures with High Frequency
body: High FrequencyPremium will be close to the potential loss
Example: Hi-end laptop worth $10,000/popular with thieves
Claim = $10,000 with probability 0.7
$0 with probability 0.3
Loading = 25% of expected loss = .25($7,000) = $1,750
Little demand for such insurance
Premium is 87.5% of potential claim

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Exposures with Correlated Losses
body: (Highly) Correlated Losses
Insurer needs to hold a large amount of capital to make promise to pay claims credible
Therefore, profit loading is high
Thus, less insurance coverage for highly correlated losses
Situations that give rise to highly correlated losses are sometimes referred to as “clash losses.”

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Exposures with Parameter Uncertainty
body: Parameter Uncertainty (aka Uncertain Expected Losses or Ambiguity): Insurer does not know or is unsure of the true expected loss
If estimate of expected loss is too low (or too high) for one policyholder, then estimate is too low (or too high) for many policyholders (insurer’s errors in predicted losses are correlated)
Parameter uncertainty causes the distribution of avg. losses around the insurer’s estimate of expected losses to have a great variance, same effect as with correlated losses
Insurer needs to hold a large amount of capital
Profit loading is high
Less insurance for exposures with significant parameter uncertainty

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Exposures with Parameter Uncertainty
body: Example
Property valued at $50,000, large number of policyholders
Probability of loss = 0.02 or 0.04
Insurer does not which probability is the true one
Insurer views each probability as equally likely
Insurer’s expected claim costs = $1,500 = (1000+2000)/2
But insurer knows that claim costs could be much higher
Insurer needs a lot of capital

BOTTOM LINE: The costs of holding larger amounts of capital imply that when it is more difficult to estimate expected losses (i.e., there is a high level of parameter uncertainty), insurance coverage will be limited
New types of riskReflects parameter uncertainty.

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Moral Hazard: An Important Limiting Factor in the Effectiveness of Insurance Markets
body: Controlling Moral Hazard Is Critical to the Viability of Insurance Markets

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Moral Hazard
body: Moral Hazard: Refers to the effect of insurance on the insured’s incentives to reduce losses
Examples
Drive less carefully, don’t lock car door when insured
Less concerned about buying a home in a flood-prone area
Consume more health care when insured
Less diligent about workplace safety
Main Point
Insurers understand insurance gives rise to moral hazard
Insurance market responds to this in several ways
But central point is that as a result of Moral Hazard, insurance contracts will generally offer less than full coverage (deductibles, co-pays) causes less than full insurance coverage
Insured will therefore have to bear some risk

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Conditions for Moral Hazard
body: Two Conditions Required for Moral Hazard
Expected losses depend on insured’s behavior
Effect of behavior on expected losses is costly to observe and measure (therefore difficult to do anything about)
Examples
Example 1: Claim costs increase with driving speed
Costly for insurers to monitor driving speed
Less so today (E. g., Usage based insurance rating – “Snapshot”)
Example 2: Health costs rise with poor diet
Costly for health insurer to monitor diet

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Implications of Moral Hazard
body: As consumers, we actually want contracts that reduce moral hazard
Otherwise, we must pay higher premiums
So, contracts are written to place some risk on the insureds to help control (disincentivize) moral hazard
This is done through the inclusion of
Deductibles (limits coverage)
Coinsurance (limits coverage)
Experience rating (penalizes incaution)

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Implications of Moral Hazard
body: Alternative: Make premium or coverage contingent on consumer’s behavior during the policy period
Progressive’s “Snapshot” device is one example of how to do this in auto insurance
John Hancock’s new interactive life insurance program
Allows you to earn premium discounts and other perks (e.g., gift cards) for those willing to use a fitness track or app that documents healthy lifestyle
There are now smartphone apps that do much the same
Retrospectively rated policies in workers compensation
IMPORTANT: Technology is helping to reduce moral hazard by rapidly reducing monitoring costs
World is become loaded with sensor devices all connected though the “Internet of Things”

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Adverse Selection: Can Also Limit Insurability
body: Adverse selection occurs when
policyholders have different expected losses
insurers cannot classifysame price charged to all
At a given price
higher risk consumers will buy more coverage
lower risk consumers will buy less coverage
Thus adverse selectionlow risk people obtain less coverage (because they have to pay too much)
Insurers recognize that risks are heterogeneous and therefore use classification
But classification is costly and only do so to the extent it is cost-efficient; Those some adverse selection persists.

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Contractual Provisions that Limit Coverage
body: Summary of Factors Limiting Demand
administrative costs
capital costs
profit loadings
moral hazard
adverse selection
Now: Contractual Provisions that Limit Coverage
deductibles
coinsurance
policy limits
exclusions

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Deductibles
body: Deductibles: Share of loss for which the policyholder is responsible. Insurer pays nothing if claim is less than equal to the deductible.
Example
policy with a $500 deductible
then policyholder pays first $500 of losses
E.g.: I’m in an auto accident and the cost to repair my car is $3,000. I pay the first $500, insurer pays $2,500
Types of deductibles
per occurrence (i.e., deductible paid on each claim)
Aggregate (i.e., total of claims must reach a specific deductible amount before coverage kicks in)

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Deductibles and Claim Processing Costs
body: Deductibles reduce cost of processing small claims
Example: Fixed claim processing cost of $200
$2,000 with probability 0.01
Loss = 100 with probability 0.10
0 with probability 0.89
Expected claim cost + claim processing cost w/o a deductible = .01(2000) + .1(100) + .01(200) + .1(200) = $30 + $22 = $52
Expected claim cost + claim processing cost w/ $100 deductible = .01($1,900) + .01($200) = $19 + $2 = $21
NOTE: now there’s a 0% chance of a $100 loss for the insurer since that amount is equal to deductible and will be paid by policyholder
Marginal cost of insuring the $100 loss equals $31 = $52-$21 (very expensive)

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Deductibles and Moral Hazard
body: Deductibles reduce moral hazard
Insureds take greater care if they have to pay part of the loss
The higher the deductible, the lower the premium
People should take the highest deductible they can afford
Many people mistakenly believe that insurers simply institute deductibles to avoid paying claims
Deductibles actually make insurance more affordable
Allow insurers to offer higher limits of coverage
Low value claims are more akin to maintenance issues and are often high frequency in nature

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Deductibles and Adverse Selection
body: Deductibles might be used to reduce adverse selection
Recall, adverse selection occurs when the insurer cannot classify, but the policyholders know their risk
At a given price
high risk people will buy more coverage
low risk will buy less coverage

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Deductibles and Adverse Selection
body: Insurer offers multiple policies with different deductibles and different prices per dollar of coverage
Lower deductible (higher coverage) policies have a higher price per dollar of coverage
Higher risk people might choose the lower deductible (higher priced) policies
Lower risk people might choose the higher deductible (lower priced) policies
Thus policyholders separate themselves into different policy groups

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Coinsurance
body: With coinsurance, insured pays a proportion (the coinsurance rate) of any loss
Example: Insured pays 20% of all medical costs
Coinsurance likely causes fewer services to be consumed (fewer claims) because the marginal cost of consumption >0

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Policy Limits
body: A policy limit is the maximum amount that the insurer will pay
In property insurance, policy limits prevent people from paying for coverage in excess of losses they could sustain (i.e., don’t want to be “overinsured”)
Limits also place an upward bound on what an insurer will need to pay. Therefore insurers can estimate maximum possible losses

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Purposes of Policy Limits
body: In addition to placing an upward bound on what an insurance allowing insurers to estimate maximum possible losses…
Reduce classification costs when consumers have information that is costly for insurers to obtain
Example: Homeowners policy limits coverage for jewelry losses to $2,500
Those with more expensive jewelry buy special coverage
Insurer does not have to investigate the value of each policyholder’s jewelry

topic: The Role of Capital in Insurance
title: Exclusions
body: Policies exclude coverage for some types of losses
Why?
Reduce administrative costs
e.g., high frequency/low severity claims
Reduce capital costs
Such as for highly correlated events: war, nuclear cont.
Reduce moral hazard
e.g., suicide excluded for first 2 years of life ins. policies
Reduce adverse selection
Non-fortuitous losses
e.g., normal wear and tear (maintenance issues)
Tailor coverage to buyers’ needs
Not all coverages in policy needed, so why pay for them
Any many exclusions can be restored for a price
