title: Legal doctrines of insurance
body: Many Important Legal Principles Govern Insurance Markets

title: Why Have Legal Doctrines?
body: Legal Doctrines of Insurance: Fundamental rules that apply to all insurance contracts (i.e., policies)
Why not allow free contracting?
Legal doctrines reduce contracting costs
Difficult to anticipate every possible contingency and to write very detailed contracts
Therefore it is useful to have principles that will be applied in a consistent manner to unforeseen circumstances
Applicable especially to the problems of
moral hazard
information disclosure
Legal system also helps resolve disputes and enforce contracts

title: Resolving Coverage Disputes
body: Insurance Contracts Are Contracts of Adhesion
Ambiguities are interpreted in favor of policyholder, who is generally viewed to be the less sophisticated party
Applied less often to commercial insurance (both parties viewed as sophisticated)
Doctrine of Reasonable Expectations
Courts interpret contracts as would a reasonable person not trained in the law
Bad Faith Suits
Law suits alleging that insurer has acted in manner inconsistent with what reasonable policyholder would have expected and therefore failed to act in good faith.
If successful, such suits sometimes lead to awards far in excess of actual damages

title: Legal Doctrines and Moral Hazard
body: Legal doctrines that mitigate moral hazard
Indemnity Principle
Insurable Interest
Subrogation

title: Principle of Indemnity
body: The insurer agrees to pay no more than the actual amount of the loss (i.e., insured should not profit from the loss)
Purpose
To prevent the insured from profiting from a loss
To reduce moral hazard

Example: If the insured has their home insured for $200,000 and sustains a partial loss of $50,000 when the home is damaged in a fire, then they should receive only $50,000 to compensate for actual damages even if policy limits are $200,000.
If the policyholder could collect more, they would be profiting from insurance
Moral hazard would potentially come into play if the policyholder could collect $200K from her $50K loss, incentivizing dishonest behavior

title: How Important Is the Principle of Indemnity?
body: So important that one of Hollywood’s greatest film classics (1944) was dedicated to the topic!
Love, fraud and MURDER!!!
Famous line from Fred MacMurray’s
character, Walter Neff, the insurance
Salesman…

Double Indemnity: is a clause or provision in a life insurance or accident policy whereby the company agrees to pay the stated multiple (e.g., double, triple) of the face amount in the contract in cases of death caused by accidental means. This includes murder by a person other than, and not in collusion with, the beneficiary of the insurance policy, and most accidental deaths. It excludes suicide, and deaths caused by the insured person's own gross negligence, as well as natural causes.

title: Actual Cash Value (ACV): Property Insurance
body: In property insurance, indemnification is often based on the actual cash value (ACV) of the property at the time of loss
3 Main Methods to Determine ACV
Replacement cost less depreciation
Fair market value: The price a willing buyer would pay a willing seller in a free market
Broad evidence rule: Means that the determination of ACV should include all relevant factors an expert would use to determine the value of the property

title: Actual Cash Value Sample Question
body: ACV = (Replacement Cost) – (Depreciation)
Example: Jerry purchased a dining room set for $5000 and insured the furniture on an actual cash value basis.
Three years later the entire set was destroyed by fire.
At the time of loss, the property had depreciated in value by 50%
The replacement cost of a new dining room set at the time of loss was $6000.
Ignoring any deductible, how much will Jerry collect from the insurer? Show your calculations.
If Jerry were paid the full replacement cost of $6000 then the principle of Indemnity would be violated because Jerry would receive the value of a new furniture even if the original was 3 years old

title: Principle of Indemnity (Continued)
body: There are some exceptions to the principle of indemnity
A valued policy pays the face amount of insurance if a total loss occurs (i.e., pays the stated value of the policy)
Some states have a valued policy law that requires payment of the face amount of insurance to the insured if a total loss to real property occurs from a peril specified in the law
Replacement cost insurance means there is no deduction for depreciation in determining the amount paid for a loss
A life insurance contract is a valued policy that pays a stated sum to the beneficiary upon the insured’s death

title: Exceptions to Principle of Indemnity
body: A valued policy pays the face amount of insurance if a total loss occurs (not too common today except for life insurance, and rare/valuable items (e.g., Picasso))
Some states have a valued policy law that requires payment of the face amount of insurance to the insured if a total loss to real property occurs from a peril specified in the law
Replacement cost insurance means there is no deduction for depreciation in determining the amount paid for a loss (becoming increasingly common)
A life insurance contract is a valued policy that pays a stated sum to the beneficiary upon the insured’s death

title: Exceptions to the Principle of Indemnity
body: Valued policies include
Antiques
Fine arts
Rare paintings (e.g., the Mona Lisa doesn’t depreciate)
Family heirlooms
Valued policy laws
Original purpose to protect insured from over-insurance
Less important now since over-insurance is rare

title: Collectibles: Insured Under Valued Policies
body: Valued policies include

title: Exceptions to the Principle of Indemnity (cont.)
body: Life Insurance
Not a contract of indemnity
Valued policy that pays a stated sum at the Insured’s death, no matter how much your life is “worth” at time of death
Usually concerned less about moral hazard in life insurance (i.e., presence of life insurance doesn’t usually contribute to behaviors that increase likelihood of death
Exception: Suicide—2-year suicide exclusion in life policies

title: Exceptions to the Principle of Indemnity (cont.)
body: Replacement Cost Insurance
No deduction taken for depreciation in determining the amount paid for a loss
Recognizes that few people budget for depreciation.
Under ACV, Insured could be required to come up with a substantial cash sum to restore the property to its original condition.

Example: Assume roof on home is 5 years old and has a useful life of 20 years. Roof is damaged by a tornado. If replacement cost of roof is $10,000, under ACV rule receive only $7,500 = ($10,000 - $2,500). But with a replacement cost policy, receive the full $10,000 (less any applicable deductible).

title: Principle of Insurable Interest
body: The insured must stand to lose financially if a loss occurs
Purpose
To prevent gambling (in the context of insurance)
To reduce moral hazard
To measure the amount of loss
Recovery shouldn’t exceed insurance interest
Example 1: Who can take a life insurance policy out on ?
Example 2: Who can take out a property insurance policy on your parents’ home?

title: Importance of Insurable Interest
body: To be legally enforceable, all insurance contracts must be supported by insurable interest.
A policy can be voided if insurable interest is found to be non-existent.

title: When Must An Insurable Interest Exist?
body: Property Insurance: at the time of the loss
Life Insurance: only at inception of the policy

title: Examples of Insurable Interest (P/L)
body: Ownership of Property
Homeowner, vehicle owner, business owner
Potential Legal Liability
Dry cleaner has insurable interest in customers’ property
Auto repair shop has insurable interest in my car while on their property
Secured Creditors
Bank granting a mortgage has an insurable interest in your home
Contractual Right
Example: Business contracting to purchase goods from abroad
Could suffer loss if goods to arrive (or arrive on time)

title: Examples of Insurable Interest (Life Ins.)
body: Insurable Interest: Not an issue when you buy life insurance on your own life
Law automatically presumes insurance interest exists when a person voluntarily purchases life insurance on their own life
Can designate anyone, any entity I want as my beneficiary.
No requirement that beneficiary demonstrate that an insurable interest exists (e.g., you could designate as your beneficiary)
Life Insurance Purchased on Another Person’s Life
Requires demonstration of insurance interest
Example 1: Familial Interest: Close family ties (husband on wife)

Example 2: Pecuniary (Financial): Corporation may insure life of CEO, top salesperson or business partner because of the negative impact this person’s death could have on the company (known as “key man” insurance)

title: Choice of Contract Type Explained By Two Factors
body: The costs of assessing value
Moral hazard

title: Principle of Subrogation
body: Substitution of the insurer in place of the insured for the purpose of claiming indemnity from a third person for a loss covered by insurance. [Insurer, after paying a claim, has the right to recover against the third party responsible for that loss (i.e., “go after” the negligent party]
Purpose
To prevent the insured from collecting twice for the same loss
To hold the negligent party responsible for the loss
To hold down insurance rates

title: Principle of Subrogation (cont.)
body: The insurer is entitled only to the amount it has paid under the policy
The insured cannot impair the insurer’s subrogation rights
Subrogation does not apply to life insurance and to most individual health insurance contracts
The insurer cannot subrogate against its own insureds

title: Subrogation Example
body: Assume a negligent motorist
Fails to stop at a red light
Smashes into the Insured’s car
Causing damages to the Insured’s car of $5,000.
Insured has purchased collision insurance for her car.
The Insured’s Insurance Company will
Pay the physical damage loss (less any deductible)
Then attempt to collect from (“go after”) the negligent motorist

title: Subrogation Example (cont.)
body: Alternatively, the Insured could attempt to collect directly from the negligent motorist.
Subrogation does not apply, if a loss payment is not made by the Insured’s insurance company.
To the extent that a loss payment is made, the Insurer receives the legal right to collect damages from the negligent third party.

title: Subrogation’s Effects
body: Prevents the Insured from collecting twice for the same loss (only the insurer can collect if the Insured has been indemnified by their insurer)
Holds the guilty party responsible
Holds down insurance rates
Generally, subrogation recoveries are factored into the rate-making process

title: Subrogation Caveats
body: The Insured cannot do anything after the loss that impairs the Insurer’s subrogation rights.
Subrogation does not apply to life insurance and most health insurance contacts.

Effect: If Joe is killed by a drunk driver and he had a $100K life insurance policy in force, his life insurance policy would pay the full $100K. However, his wife would still be free to sue the negligent driver for damages because of his unlawful death.
Insurer cannot recover against its own Insureds.
Would defeat the purpose of insurance

title: Case Application—Insurable Interest
body: Background
Jeff is a book dealer who purchased a building from Richard. Jeff obtained a loan from the Gateway Bank, which held a mortgage on the building.
Jeff planned to store his inventory of books in the building. He also planned to use part of the building for a fast food restaurant.
When Jeff applied for property insurance on the building, he did not tell the agent about the fast food restaurant because premiums would be substantially higher.
Eight months after the policy was issued, a fire occurred in the restaurant that caused substantial damage to the entire building

title: Case Application – Insurable Interest (cont.)
body: Do any of the following parties have an insurable interest in the building at the time of loss?
Jeff
Richard
Gateway Bank

Richard told Jeff he could save money by taking over Richard’s policy instead of purchasing a new policy. Can Richard validly “assign” his existing property insurance policy to Jeff without notifying the insurer?

title: Case Application
body: Could Jeff’s insurer deny coverage for the fire loss based on material concealment?
Investigation of the fire revealed that an electrician wired an electrical outlet in the restaurant, which caused the fire. Explain how subrogation might apply in this case.

title: Legal Doctrines & Information Disclosure
body: Legal doctrines about inducing truthful information disclosure
Utmost good faith
Misrepresentations
Concealment

title: Principle of Utmost Good Faith
body: A higher degree of honesty is imposed on both parties to an insurance contract than is imposed on parties to other contracts
Insurance contracts are contracts uberrimae fidei, or of the utmost good faith.
Neither party is to take advantage of the other’s lack of information.
Note: This is important because one party often has more information than the other. Many provisions in insurance seek to remedy these informational asymmetries.

title: Historical Roots: Utmost Good Faith
body: Legal foundation rests on principles established long ago for insurance on ocean vessels and cargoes
Marine underwriter had to place great faith in the statements made by the applicant for insurance
The cargo may not have been able to be visually inspected
Captain/crew unknown to insurer
The contract may have been issued in a location far removed from the cargo or ship
Thus, a high degree of honesty was imposed on the applicant for insurance

title: Principle of Utmost Good Faith (cont.)
body: Supported by three legal doctrines
Representations are statements made by the applicant for insurance
A contract is voidable if the representation is material, false, and relied upon by the insurer
An innocent misrepresentation of a material fact, if relied on by the insurer, makes the contract voidable

title: Principle of Utmost Good Faith Supporting Legal Doctrines
body: A concealment is intentional failure of the applicant for insurance to reveal a material fact to the insurer
Concealment is the same thing as a nondisclosure
A warranty is a statement that becomes part of the insurance contract and is guaranteed by the maker to be true in all respects
Statements made by applicants are considered representations, not warranties

A statement achieves the status of a warranty if it becomes a condition of the insurer’s promise (e.g., if the applicant states none of the cargo vessels they want to insure contains toxic substances and the insurance contract then states coverage applies only if losses do not involve toxic substancewarranty)

title: Breach of Warranty
body: If a statement is construed as a warranty, the court may allow the insurer to deny coverage, if there has not been literal compliance with the statement
Effect can be very harsh.
To deny coverage, all the insurer may need to show was that the warranty was breached, even if the breach were unintentional and not related to the loss.

title: Elements of Misrepresentation
body: Material means that
If the Insurer knew the true facts, the policy would not have been issued or would have been issued on different terms.
False means that
The statement is not true or is misleading.
Reliance means that
The Insurer relies on the misrepresentation in issuing the policy at a specified premium

title: Concealment: Legal Significance
body: Concealment is the same as non-disclosure.
The applicant for insurance deliberately withholds material information from the insurer
Legal significance is the same as misrepresentation
The contract is voidable at the Insurer’s option.

title: Concealment Generally Has Two Elements
body: The concealed fact was known by the Insured to be material
The Insured intended to defraud the Insurer

title: Requirements of an Insurance Contract
body: To be legally enforceable, an insurance contract must meet 4 requirements
Offer and acceptance of the terms of the contract
Exchange of Consideration – the value that each party gives to the other
Competent parties, with legal capacity to enter into a binding contract
The contract must exist for a legal purpose

title: Distinct Legal Characteristics of Insurance Contracts
body: An insurance contract is
Aleatory: values exchanged are not equal
Unilateral: only the insurer makes a legally enforceable promise (the policyholder can walk away at any time)
Conditional: policyowner must comply with all policy provisions to collect for a covered loss (e.g., provide documentation of loss)
Personal: property insurance policy cannot be validly assigned to another party without the insurer's consent
A contract of adhesion: the insured must accept the entire contract with all of its terms and conditions

title: Distinct Legal Characteristics of Insurance Contracts (Continued)
body: Courts have ruled that any ambiguities or uncertainties in the contract are construed against the insurer (policy of adhesion).
The principle of reasonable expectations states that an insured is entitled to coverage under a policy that he or she reasonably expects it to provide, regardless of policy provisions.

title: Law and the Insurance Agent
body: An agent is someone who has the authority to act on behalf of a principal (the insurer)
Several laws govern the actions of agents and their relationship to insureds
There is no presumption of an agency relationship
An agent must be authorized to represent the principal
A principal is responsible for the acts of agents acting within the scope of their authority
Limitations can be placed on the powers of agents

title: Law and the Insurance Agent (Continued)
body: An agent’s authority comes from three sources
Express authority
Implied authority
Apparent authority
Knowledge of the agent is presumed to be knowledge of the principal with respect to matters within the scope of the agency relationship
Insurers can place limitations on the power of agents by adding a nonwaiver clause to the application or policy

title: Law and the Insurance Agent (Continued)
body: The doctrines of waiver and estoppel may require an insurer to pay a claim that it ordinarily would not have to pay
Waiver is defined as the voluntary relinquishment of a known legal right
Estoppel is the loss of a legal defense because of previous actions that are now inconsistent with that defense

Example: If an applicant for health insurance discloses a health problem and the agent assures the applicant that the condition need not be disclosed—then the insurer could be “estopped” from denying benefits based on the assertion that the information was not included on the application.

title: Basic Parts of an Insurance Contract
body: Declarations are statements that provide information about the particular property or activity to be insured
Usually on the first page of the policy
In property insurance, it contains name of the insured, location of property, period of protection, amount of insurance, premium and deductible information
Insurance contracts typically contain a page or section of definitions
For example, the insured is referred to as “you,” insurer as “we”…

title: Basic Parts of an Insurance Contract: Sample Declarations Page—Auto Policy
Basic Parts of an Insurance Contract: Sample Declarations Page—Auto Policy II
Basic Parts of an Insurance Contract (Continued)
body: Insuring Agreement summarizes the major promises of the insurer
The 2 basic forms of an insuring agreement in property insurance are
Named Perils Coverage: where only those perils specifically named in the policy are covered. If the peril is not named, it’s not covered
Open-Perils, All-Perils, All Risks or Special Coverage: where all losses are covered except those losses specifically excluded

title: Basic Parts of an Insurance Contract (Continued): II
body: Insurance contracts contain 3 major types of exclusions
Excluded Perils: e.g., in home insurance: flood, war, nuclear contamination, intentional acts (e.g., arson) [Need to buy specific coverage separately (e.g., flood insurance)]

Excluded Losses: e.g., a professional liability loss is excluded in the homeowners policy (i.e., if you operate a business out of your home, your HO policy will likely exclude any losses that arise from the operation of that business; If you’re an Uber driver, that activity is excluded under your personal auto policy)
Excluded Property: e.g., pets are not covered as personal property in the homeowners policy, neither are trees and shrubs, automobiles or aircraft…

title: Basic Parts of an Insurance Contract: III
body: Conditions are provisions in the policy that qualify or place limitations on the insurer’s promise to perform

If policy conditions are not met, the insurer can refuse to pay the claim (e.g., for a life insurance claim, need to furnish proof of death; for business interruption claim, need to demonstrate that there was actual physical loss or damage from a covered peril that gave rise to the interruption; for auto liability need to submit to physical exam if claiming injury)
Insurance policies contain a variety of miscellaneous provisions
e.g., cancellation, subrogation, grace period, misstatement of age

title: Definition of “Insured”
body: An insurance contract must identify the persons or parties who are insured under the policy
The named insured is the person or persons named in the declarations section of the policy
The first named insured has certain additional rights and responsibilities that do not apply to other named insureds (e.g., responsible for payment of premium but right to receive premium refund)

Other Insureds: A policy may cover other parties even though they are not specifically named (e.g., in an HO policy, resident relatives under the age of 21 under the care of the insured; resident relatives of the insured under age 24 who are FT students away from home)
Additional insureds may be added using an endorsement (e.g., if I lease my land to a tenant farmer, I might want to be listed on tenant’s farm liability policy as an additional insured

title: Endorsements and Riders
body: If coverage is excluded in a policy, it can sometimes be restored through and endorsement or rider
Endorsement: In property and liability insurance, an endorsement is a written provision that adds to, deletes from, or modifies the provisions in the original contract
e.g., an earthquake endorsement to a homeowners policy
A livery driver endorsement is necessary to use personal vehicle to be insured for use of your personal auto as an Uber driver
Rider: In life and health insurance, a rider is a provision that amends or changes the original policy
e.g., a waiver-of-premium rider on a life insurance policy

title: Deductibles
body: A deductible is a provision by which a specified amount is subtracted from the total loss payment that otherwise would be payable
The purpose of a deductible is to
Eliminate small claims that are expensive to handle and process
Reduce premiums paid by the insured
Reduce moral hazard (puts policyholder’s “skin in the game”)

title: Deductibles (Continued)
body: Straight Deductible: the insured must pay a certain number of dollars of loss before the insurer is required to make a payment (usually takes the form of a fixed dollar amount, such as $1,000, but could also be a share of the insured value of the property, such as 2%)
e.g., an auto insurance deductible usually has a fixed dollar deductible of $500 to $1,000
Aggregate Deductible: means that all losses that occur during a specified time period, usually a year, are accumulated to satisfy the deductible amount

title: Deductibles in Health Insurance
body: A calendar-year deductible is a type of aggregate deductible that is found in basic medical expense and major medical insurance contracts
An elimination (waiting) period is a stated period of time at the beginning of a loss during which no insurance benefits are paid
e.g., disability income contracts that replace part of a disabled worker’s earnings typically have elimination periods of 30, 60, or 90 days, or longer periods.

title: Coinsurance
body: A coinsurance clause in a property insurance contract encourages the insured to insure the property to a stated percentage of its insurable value
If the coinsurance requirement is not met at the time of the loss, the insured must share in the loss as a coinsurer

title: Coinsurance (Continued)
body: The fundamental purpose of coinsurance is to achieve equity in rating
A property owner wishing to insure for a total loss would pay an inequitable premium if other property owners only insure for partial losses
If the coinsurance requirement is met, the insured receives a rate discount, and the policyowner who is underinsured is penalized through application of the coinsurance formula

title: Coinsurance in PROPERTY INSURANCE
body: A coinsurance clause in a property insurance contract encourages the insured to insure the property to a stated percentage of its insurable value (e.g. at least 80%)
Insurance-to-Value (ITV) is the goal
If the coinsurance requirement is not met at the time of the loss, the insured must share in the loss as a coinsurer
In other words, there is a penalty for not insuring to the appropriate property value. The penalty is assessed per this formula: (DID/SHOULD) x Loss = Amt. of Recovery, or stated differently

title: Coinsurance in Property Insurance II
body: The purpose of coinsurance is to achieve equity in rating
A property owner wishing to insure for a total loss would pay an inequitable premium if other property owners only insure for partial losses
If the coinsurance requirement is met, the insured receives a rate discount, and the policyowner who is underinsured is penalized through application of the coinsurance formula
Also helps reduce likelihood of underinsurance (i.e., policyholder receives payout from insurer insufficient to fully recover).
Underinsurance has sometimes led to litigation, with policyholders accusing insurers of not selling a policy with adequate limits

title: Property Coinsurance: Example
body: Commercial Building
80% coinsurance clause
Required Amount of Insurance = $400,000 = 0.8($500,000)
Insurance Amount Actually Purchased = $300,000
300,000 x $10,000 = $7,500 = Amount of Recovery
If this were a “replacement cost” policy, you would simply use the replacement cost figure instead of ACV

title: Insurance-to-Value and Coinsurance
body: C = amount of coverage purchased (policy limit) = “Did”
L = the amount of the loss = “Loss”
V = value of property at the time of the loss
r = coinsurance rate
Value x coinsurance rate = “Should”
Insurer will pay the lesser of C , L
Loss Insurer pays

title: Remember
body: Purpose of Coinsurance in Property Insurance
To achieve equity in rating
Why? Because, most property losses are partial
“Insurance-to-Value” (ITV)
Another name for “Coinsurance” in Property Insurance

title: Coinsurance in Health Insurance
body: Health insurance policies frequently contain a coinsurance clause (Note: very different from property coinsurance)
The clause requires the insured to pay a specified percentage of covered medical expenses in excess of the deductible
The purposes of coinsurance in health insurance are to reduce premiums and prevent overutilization of policy benefits

Example: Bob’s health plan requires him to pay a deductible of $50 for each visit to the doctor and contains a coinsurance clause requiring him to pay 20% of all charges above that amount. If Bob has a procedure that costs $500, how much is paid by Bob and how much by his insurer?
ANSWER: Bob pays his $50 deductible + 20% of the remaining $450, or $90. So Bob pays a total of $140 and his insurer pays $360.

title: Deeper Look At Coinsurance in HEALTH INSURANCE
body: With coinsurance, insured pays a proportion (the coinsurance rate) of any loss
Example: Insured pays 20% of all medical costs (usually after a deductible applies as well)
Bob feels sick and goes to see the doctor. The charges from the doctor are $200. Bob has a $50 per visit deductible. How much does Bob pay? $50 + .2($150) = $50 + $30 = $80
Reason for coinsurance provisions
Insureds demand less than full insurance when the policy has a loading
If Bob had a 5% coinsurance provision he would only pay $57.50 and might be tempted to visit the doctor sooner or more often.
Reduce moral hazard

title: Other-Insurance Provisions
body: The purpose of other-insurance provisions is to prevent profiting from insurance and violating the principle of indemnity. This is necessary because it is not uncommon for more than one insurance policy to apply to a given claim (because no single insurer wants to be overexposed to the risk). The question is therefore how should the loss be divided up among the various insurers in a way that is consistent with the principle of indemnity. 2 Methods
Under a pro rata liability provision, each insurer’s share of the loss is based on the proportion that its insurance bears to the total amount of insurance on the property

Under contribution by equal shares, each insurer shares equally in the loss until the share paid by each insurer equals the lowest limit of liability under any policy, or until the full amount of the loss is paid

title: Exhibit 10.3 Pro Rata Liability Example
body: Note: It’s very common for multiple insurers to jointly insure a single risk (e.g., building), so they need to agree in advance how to apportion any losses that occur.
Example: Suppose Bob owns a building valued at $500K. Insurance Company A agrees to write $300K of coverage, B writes 100K and C writes $100K. How much is paid by each company if a $100K loss occurs?

title: Exhibit 10.4 Contribution by Equal Shares (Example 1)
body: Example: Suppose Bob owns a building valued at $500K. Insurance Company A agrees to write $100K of coverage, B writes 200K and C writes $300K. How much is paid by each company if a $150K loss occurs?

title: Exhibit 10.5 Contribution by Equal Shares (Example 2)
body: Example: Suppose Bob owns a building valued at $600K. Insurance Company A agrees to write $100K of coverage, B writes 200K and C writes $300K. How much is paid by each company if a $500K loss occurs?
Each insurer pays equal amounts until policy limits are exhausted

title: Other-insurance Provisions II
body: Under a primary and excess insurance provision, the primary insurer pays first, and the excess insurer pays only after the policy limits under the primary policy are exhausted
Example 1: Once coverage under one of a business’s standard liability policy is exhausted, it may have a separate excess policy that kicks in

Example 2: If you’re driving a friend’s car (with their permission) and you’re in an accident and found to be at fault, the insurance on the vehicle is primary (insurance follows the vehicle). If the limits on the friend’s car are exhausted, your insurance kicks in as “excess”

The coordination of benefits provision in group health insurance is designed to prevent overinsurance and the duplication of benefits if one person is covered under more than one group health insurance plan

title: Other-insurance Provisions (Continued)
body: Many states have adopted part or all of the coordination of benefits provisions developed by the NAIC, which include a number of rules, including
Coverage as an employee is usually primary to coverage as a dependent
For dependents in families where the parents are married or are not separated, the plan of the parent whose birthday occurs first during the year is primary

title: Pro Rata and Excess Coverage Clauses
body: Issue: How is coverage divided when multiple policies apply to the same loss
Pro rata clause: divide in proportion to amount of coverage
Excess clause: one policy pays losses in excess of the other policy’s limit
Why have these clauses?
Protects the principle of indemnity (can’t be doubly indemnified)
Prevents coverage in excess of loss, which would cause moral hazard

title: Indemnity vs. Valued Contracts
body: Indemnity Contract: Insurer pays based on the amount of loss that occurred (i.e., amount is determined after loss occurs)
Example: auto physical damage (adjuster examines damage and determines repair costs)
Valued contract: Insurer pays a pre-determined amount
Example: life insurance (value of death benefit is determined when policy is purchased)

title: Choice of Indemnity vs. Valued Contract
body: Type of contract is largely explained by
The costs of assessing value
Moral hazard
When the amount of loss can be assessed at low cost following the loss, more likely to have indemnity contracts

When moral hazard (i.e., insured’s behavior is not greatly influenced by the existence of insurance) is less likely to be a problem, fixing the insurance payment (in dollar terms) before a loss can avoid costly haggling following a loss (e.g., life insurance)

title: Exclusions
body: Policies exclude coverage for some types of losses
Why?
reduce administrative costs
reduce capital costs
reduce moral hazard
reduce adverse selection

title: Exclusions
body: Insurance contracts contain three major types of exclusions
Excluded perils, e.g., flood, intentional act (arson)
Excluded losses, e.g., a professional liability loss is excluded in the homeowners policy
Excluded property, e.g., pets, trees and shrubs are not covered as personal property in the homeowners policy

title: Exclusions Are Necessary
body: Because
Some perils are not commercially insurable
e.g., catastrophic losses due to war
Extraordinary hazards are present
Using your personal automobile as a taxi service (such as Ubermaterially increases risk)
Regularly renting our your home through Airbnb (turns your home into a hotelmaterially increases risk)
Coverage is provided by other contracts
e.g., use of auto excluded on homeowners policy

title: Exclusions Are Necessary (cont’d)
body: Because
Moral hazard is present or it would be difficult to measure the amount of loss
e.g., coverage of money (i.e., cash) limited to $200 in homeowners policy
Coverage not needed by typical insureds
e.g., homeowners policy does not cover aircraft, watercraft

title: Coverage Not Needed by Typical Insureds
body: Homeowners example
Most Homeowners policies do not cover aircraft as personal property
To cover aircraft would be unfair to most insureds, who do not own aircraft
Because premiums would have to be substantially higher

title: Examples: Perils Not Commercially Insurable
body: The peril may depart substantially from the requirements of an insurable risk
Property and liability contracts
Exposure to nuclear radiation
Health Insurance
Losses within the direct control of the insured
For example, intentional, self-inflicted injury
Property Insurance
Wear and tear (ordinary maintenance issue)
Inherent vice (damage without external force, e. g., fruit to rot, diamonds to crack)

title: Moral Hazard Present
body: Homeowners example
Coverage for money typically limited to $200
If unlimited amounts were provided, fraudulent claims would increase.
Also, there would be a problem determining the exact amount of loss.

title: Extraordinary Hazards Involved
body: Taxi Cab Example - Personal auto policy
Personal Auto policy assumes vehicle used for personal or recreational purposes
If car is used as a taxicab, the chance of an accident and resulting lawsuit is much higher
Could result in inadequate premium, or
Unfair rate discrimination against other insureds who are not using the policy as a taxicab

title: Coverage Provided by Other Contracts
body: Homeowners policy example
Excludes coverage for autos because should be covered under Personal Auto Policy
Commercial General Liability policy example
Excludes claims covered under Workers Compensation policy

title: Endorsements and Riders
body: If coverage is excluded in a policy, it can sometimes be restored through and endorsement or rider
Endorsement: In property and liability insurance, an endorsement is a written provision that adds to, deletes from, or modifies the provisions in the original contract
e.g., an earthquake endorsement to a homeowners policy
A livery driver endorsement is necessary to use personal vehicle to be insured for use of your personal auto as an Uber driver
Rider: In life and health insurance, a rider is a provision that amends or changes the original policy
e.g., a waiver-of-premium rider on a life insurance policy

title: Impact of /Restrictions on Endorsements
body: Endorsement generally takes precedence over the conflicting terms of the contract
However, if a law or regulation requires that a standard policy contain certain provisions
An endorsement cannot be used to circumvent the purpose of legislation
If an endorsement is contrary to a law or regulation, the policy is read as if the endorsement did not exist

title: “Other” Insurance Provisions
body: Typically present in Property & Liability and Health Insurance policies
Apply when more than one contract covers the same loss.
Purpose: To prevent profiting from insurance and violation of the principle indemnity

title: Types of Other Insurance Provisions
body: Pro Rata Liability: Each insurer’s share of the loss is based on the proportion that its insurance bears of the total amount of insurance on the property

Contribution by Equal Shares: Each insurer shares equally in the loss until the share paid by each insurer equals the lowest limit of liability under any policy, or until the full amount of the loss is paid
Primary and Excess Insurance: The “primary” insurer pays first, and the excess insurer pays only after the policy limits of the primary policy are exhausted.

title: Pro Rata Liability: Example
body: Pro Rata Liability: Each insurer’s share of the loss is based on the proportion that its insurance bears of the total amount of insurance on the property.

Example: Because many insurers limit the amount of coverage they write on any given policy, three insurers cooperate to insurance a property insured for $500,000. Company A agrees to write $300,000; Company B $100,000; Company C $100,000K

title: Contribution by Equal Shares: (Example 1)
body: Contribution by Equal Shares: Each insurer shares equally in the loss until the share paid by each insurer equals the lowest limit of liability under any policy, or until the full amount of the loss is paid.
Example: Suppose the loss is $150,000Each insurer would pay an equal share of $50,000 because that share is below the amount of insurance underwritten by all 3 insurers

title: Contribution by Equal Shares: (Example 2)
body: Contribution by Equal Shares: Each insurer shares equally in the loss until the share paid by each insurer equals the lowest limit of liability under any policy, or until the full amount of the loss is paid.
Example: Suppose the loss is $500,000Insurer A’s insurance (limit) is exhausted at $100,000. Insurer B and C will need to pay the remaining loss so that the full loss is paid (hence $200,000 each)

title: Primary and Excess Provisions
body: Under a primary and excess insurance provision, the primary insurer pays first.
The excess insurer pays only after the policy limits under the primary policy are exhausted.

title: Primary and Excess Insurance Personal Auto Example
body: Bob borrows Jill’s car and is the at-fault driver in an accident
Limits of Liability
Bob: $100,000 per person for Bodily Injury (BI)
Jill: $50,000 per person for BI
Normal rule: Liability insurance on borrowed vehicle is primary; any other insurance is considered excess
Court judgment of $75,000
Jill’s policy is primary; pays $50,000
Bob’s policy is excess; pays $25,000

title: Resolving Coverage Disputes
body: Contracts of Adhesion
Ambiguities are interpreted in favor of policyholder
Applied less often to commercial insurance
Doctrine of Reasonable Expectations
Courts interpret contracts as would a reasonable person not trained in the law
Mediation/Arbitration
May be required before litigation can be filed
Bad Faith Suits

title: Liability System in the United States
body: From a risk management standpoint, the U.S. liability system poses a significant threat.
US liability system is by far the costliest in the world
The extent and size of liability exposures has increased dramatically since the post World War II years.
Understanding the legal liability system has become an essential competency.

Liability premiums in China account for a much smaller share of GDP
Liability insurance premiums totaled $160 billion in 2013, accounting for 0.26% of global GDP. This also equates to 10% of global non-life premiums and 23% of global commercial lines premiums
